cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a null pointer dereference vulnerability in the gf_isom_get_payt_count function in hint_track.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service.

undesired_operation.description: hmhd is dereferenced (hmhd->type) before the null check (!hmhd) that appears later, causing a null pointer dereference if hmhd is NULL.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (null pointer dereference). Potentially exploitable depending on platform memory layout.

step_by_step_execution.description: hmhd is assigned from trak->Media->information->InfoHeader, which is NULL

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: hmhd is NULL, so dereferencing hmhd->type causes a null pointer dereference before the condition can be evaluated

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE: a null pointer dereference of hmhd before the null check. The fix adds !hmhd to the first condition, which is precisely what was changed in the commit.

actual_issue_summary: In GetHintFormat(), hmhd (derived from InfoHeader) is dereferenced via hmhd->type before being checked for NULL. The fix adds a !hmhd check before dereferencing hmhd->type, preventing a null pointer dereference that could cause a denial of service crash.

cve_desc: Depending on the way the format strings in the card label are crafted it's possible to leak kernel stack memory. There is also the possibility for DoS due to the v4l2loopback kernel module crashing when providing the card label on request (reproduce e.g. with many %s modifiers in a row).

undesired_operation.description: dev->card_label is passed directly as the format string argument to snprintf, allowing format string specifiers within the label (e.g. %s, %x, %n) to read/write stack memory
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of stack/kernel memory via %x/%p specifiers, or potential arbitrary kernel memory write via %n specifier, leading to privilege escalation or denial of service

state.value: User-controlled string potentially containing format specifiers like %s, %x, %n

initial_state.value: User-controlled card label string that may contain format specifiers
Uninitialized output buffer in v4l2_capability struct

step_by_step_execution.description: labellen is computed as the minimum of sizeof(cap->card) and sizeof(dev->card_label)
snprintf is called with dev->card_label directly as the format string, without an explicit "%s" format specifier

out_state.value: contains formatted output interpreting dev->card_label as a format string, leaking stack values

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-2652: dev->card_label is used as the format string in snprintf instead of being passed as a "%s" argument, enabling format string attacks (CWE-134). The fix adds the explicit "%s" format specifier.

actual_issue_summary: In vidioc_querycap, dev->card_label (user-controllable) was passed directly as the format string to snprintf. A crafted card label containing format specifiers (%s, %x, etc.) could leak kernel stack memory or cause a kernel crash (DoS). The fix adds an explicit "%s" format specifier.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. TensorFlow's type inference can cause a heap out of bounds read as the bounds checking is done in a `DCHECK` (which is a no-op during production). An attacker can control the `input_idx` variable such that `ix` would be larger than the number of values in `node_t.args`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. This is the only affected version.

undesired_operation.description: node_t.args(ix) is called with an index ix that may be >= node_t.args_size(), because the bounds check is only a DCHECK (debug-only assertion) which is compiled out in release/optimized builds. This results in an out-of-bounds read on the protobuf repeated field.
code_snippets:
DCHECK(ix < node_t.args_size())
            << "input " << i << " should have an output " << ix
            << " but instead only has " << node_t.args_size()
            << " outputs: " << node_t.DebugString();
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read on a protobuf repeated field. Depending on the protobuf implementation, this can lead to a crash (denial of service) or information disclosure by reading memory beyond the args array. In TensorFlow's threat model, a crafted graph with mismatched output indices can trigger this.

initial_state.value: vector initialized to 0s, then populated from edge->src_output()

step_by_step_execution.description: input_idx is populated from edges' src_output() values

out_state.value: vector of src_output() values from edges

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The input node has a non-trivial type set

step_by_step_execution.description: ix is assigned from input_idx[i]

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: DCHECK is a no-op in production builds, so this check is never evaluated

step_by_step_execution.description: node_t.args(ix) is called with out-of-bounds index, causing heap OOB read

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-23592: the DCHECK-only bounds check on ix before accessing node_t.args(ix) allows an OOB read in production builds.

actual_issue_summary: The DCHECK guarding ix < node_t.args_size() is a no-op in production builds, allowing an attacker to supply a crafted graph where input_idx values exceed the number of args in node_t, causing an out-of-bounds read when node_t.args(ix) is accessed. The fix replaces the DCHECK with a proper runtime bounds check that logs a warning and returns early.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `GetInitOp` is vulnerable to a crash caused by dereferencing a null pointer. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The inner find on the outputs() map is not checked against end(). If the outputs map of the signature does not contain `kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey`, the iterator returned is end(), and dereferencing it (`->second.name()`) is undefined behavior, leading to an out-of-bounds read/crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure. An attacker who controls the saved model protobuf can craft a MetaGraphDef whose signature_def contains kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey at the top level but omits it from the outputs sub-map, triggering the dereference of an invalid iterator.

state.value: valid iterator (key found in signature_def map)
end() iterator (key NOT found in outputs map)

initial_state.value: does NOT contain kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey as a key

step_by_step_execution.description: sig_def_map is set to meta_graph_def.signature_def()

out_state.value: reference to signature_def map containing kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey

step_by_step_execution.description: init_op_sig_it is set to find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) in signature_def

out_state.value: valid iterator pointing to the signature entry

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The signature_def map contains kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey, so the iterator is valid

step_by_step_execution.description: Calls init_op_sig_it->second.outputs().find(kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey) which returns end() because the outputs sub-map does not contain that key, then dereferences it via ->second.name()

in_state.value: map that does NOT contain kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey

out_state.value: end() iterator — dereferencing causes null pointer dereference / undefined behavior

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely matches CVE-2022-23577: the inner find() result on the outputs map is not checked against end(), leading to dereferencing an invalid iterator (null pointer dereference). The fix adds exactly this check.

actual_issue_summary: In GetInitOp, after finding kSavedModelInitOpSignatureKey in the top-level signature_def map, the code performs a second find on the outputs sub-map but never checks whether that key exists there. If it doesn't, the end() iterator is dereferenced, causing a null pointer dereference crash.

cve_desc: uWebSockets 18.11.0 and 18.12.0 has a stack-based buffer overflow in uWS::TopicTree::trimTree (called from uWS::TopicTree::unsubscribeAll). NOTE: the vendor's position is that this is "a minor issue or not even an issue at all" because the developer of an application (that uses uWebSockets) should not be allowing the large number of triggered topics to accumulate

undesired_operation.description: triggeredTopics is a fixed-size array of 64 elements, but the bounds check at function entry (numTriggeredTopics == 64) only fires once before the loop. Inside the loop, each iteration can increment numTriggeredTopics via triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] for terminatingWildcardChild, plus the recursive publish() call for wildcardChild can further increment it. After the loop, another triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] occurs. This allows writing past the end of the 64-element array.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds write past the triggeredTopics[64] array, potentially leading to memory corruption, denial of service, or code execution.

state.value: >=64 after multiple increments within the loop without intervening drain()

initial_state.value: any value 0..64; if 64, drain() resets to 0 at function entry

step_by_step_execution.description: At function entry, if numTriggeredTopics == 64, drain() is called resetting it to 0. Otherwise no check.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The topic contains multiple '/' separated segments, so the loop iterates multiple times

relevant_state.value: position of next '/' in topic, not npos

step_by_step_execution.description: In each loop iteration, if iterator->terminatingWildcardChild exists and is not yet triggered, triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] writes to the array and increments the counter
Recursive call publish(iterator->wildcardChild, ...) can further increment numTriggeredTopics multiple times without any bounds check

out_state.value: N+1+K where K is number of topics triggered in recursive call

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: After multiple loop iterations and recursive calls, numTriggeredTopics can exceed 64 since the drain() check only happened once at function entry

step_by_step_execution.description: triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] writes beyond the 64-element array boundary

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely identifies the CVE-2020-36406 vulnerability: the drain() check at function entry is insufficient because multiple triggeredTopics increments can occur within the loop and via recursion, causing a stack-based buffer overflow of the fixed-size 64-element triggeredTopics array. The fix moves the drain() check to immediately before each triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++] write.

actual_issue_summary: The drain() call that prevents overflow of the fixed-size triggeredTopics[64] array was placed only at the start of publish(), but the loop body and recursive calls can increment numTriggeredTopics multiple times past 64 without checking bounds, causing a stack-based buffer overflow. The fix moves the bounds check to immediately before each write to triggeredTopics[numTriggeredTopics++].

cve_desc: Glewlwyd 2.0.0, fixed in 2.6.1 is affected by an incorrect access control vulnerability. One user can attempt to log in as another user without its password.

undesired_operation.description: When password authentication fails, the code still calls user_session_update with the attacker-supplied username and an authenticated flag of 1, updating the existing server-side session to associate it with the target username as authenticated, despite the failed credential check.
undesired_operation.impact: Authentication bypass — an attacker who already possesses a valid session cookie can send a login request with a victim's username and any wrong password. The server returns 401 but still updates the server-side session to associate it with the victim's username (authenticated=1). Subsequent requests using that session cookie are treated as authenticated under the victim's identity.

initial_state.value: JSON object with username=<victim> and password=<wrong_password>
valid existing session cookie from attacker

step_by_step_execution.description: j_param is parsed from request body containing username and password

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: username is a non-empty string
No scheme_type provided, so password auth path is taken
password is a non-empty string

step_by_step_execution.description: auth_check_user_credentials is called and returns G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED because password is wrong

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Credential check failed, j_result is G_ERROR_UNAUTHORIZED

step_by_step_execution.description: get_session_id returns existing session_uid from attacker's cookie

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker has a valid session cookie, so get_session_id returns a non-NULL value

step_by_step_execution.description: user_session_update is called with the victim's username and authenticated=1, updating the attacker's session to be associated with the victim as authenticated

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the CVE: on failed password authentication, user_session_update is called with the attacker-supplied username and authenticated=1, allowing session hijack. The fix removes exactly this code block. This matches CVE-2021-45379's description of one user being able to log in as another without their password.

actual_issue_summary: When password authentication fails (wrong password), the vulnerable code still calls user_session_update with the requested username and authenticated=1 on the existing session. This allows an attacker with a valid session cookie to send a login request with a victim's username and any wrong password, and despite getting a 401 response, their session gets updated to be authenticated as the victim. The fix removes the user_session_update call from the authentication failure path.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. Under certain scenarios, Grappler component of TensorFlow can trigger a null pointer dereference. There are 2 places where this can occur, for the same malicious alteration of a `SavedModel` file (fixing the first one would trigger the same dereference in the second place). First, during constant folding, the `GraphDef` might not have the required nodes for the binary operation. If a node is missing, the correposning `mul_*child` would be null, and the dereference in the subsequent line would be incorrect. We have a similar issue during `IsIdentityConsumingSwitch`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: graph.GetNode(tensor_id.node()) may return nullptr if the node name from the input is not found in the graph, and the result is dereferenced without a null check in IsSwitch(*input_node)
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (null pointer dereference). An attacker who can supply a crafted graph where an Identity node's input references a non-existent node name can trigger a segfault.

initial_state.value: an Identity or IdentityN node with at least one input referencing a non-existent node name

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The node is an Identity node with at least one input, so the outer condition is satisfied.

step_by_step_execution.description: tensor_id is parsed from node.input(0)

out_state.value: parsed TensorId with node name referencing a non-existent graph node

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The tensor_id is a normal (non-control) input, so this check returns false and we do not return early.

step_by_step_execution.description: input_node is looked up via graph.GetNode(tensor_id.node()), which returns nullptr because the referenced node does not exist in the graph
input_node is dereferenced in IsSwitch(*input_node), causing a null pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches one of the two null pointer dereference locations described in CVE-2022-23589 (the IsIdentityConsumingSwitch case). The CWE, root cause, and fix all align perfectly.

actual_issue_summary: In TensorFlow's Grappler component, the function IsIdentityConsumingSwitch (and a similar location in constant folding) dereferences the return value of graph.GetNode() without checking for nullptr. If a SavedModel is maliciously altered so that a referenced node is missing from the graph, this leads to a null pointer dereference and crash. The fix adds a nullptr check before dereferencing.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `AddManySparseToTensorsMap` is vulnerable to an integer overflow which results in a `CHECK`-fail when building new `TensorShape` objects (so, an assert failure based denial of service). We are missing some validation on the shapes of the input tensors as well as directly constructing a large `TensorShape` with user-provided dimensions. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The overflow check uses an `int` (32-bit) variable `new_num_elements` to store the result of `MultiplyWithoutOverflow`, which returns `int64_t`. When the product fits in 64 bits but not 32 bits, the value is silently truncated. A truncated positive value bypasses the `< 0` check, allowing construction of a TensorShape with dimensions whose total number of elements overflows, leading to downstream memory issues (out-of-bounds access, denial of service).
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service or potential memory corruption — attacker provides a sparse tensor shape whose dimensions overflow when multiplied as int64_t but truncate to a benign-looking 32-bit int, bypassing the overflow check and allowing excessively large allocations or inconsistent tensor metadata.

state.value: truncated 32-bit value that appears non-negative despite actual 64-bit overflow

initial_state.value: unset (declared later as int, initialized to 1)
user-provided int64_t vector with large dimension values

step_by_step_execution.description: new_num_elements is declared as int (32-bit) and initialized to 1
MultiplyWithoutOverflow returns int64_t product of new_num_elements and input_shape_vec(i). The result is implicitly truncated to int when stored.

out_state.value: truncated 32-bit value of (1 * 2^40), which wraps to 0

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The truncated value can be 0 or positive despite the actual product being huge, so the overflow check is bypassed.

step_by_step_execution.description: TensorShape is constructed directly from input_shape_vec without proper validation, triggering a CHECK-fail internally due to overflow in TensorShape construction.

in_state.value: contains large dimensions that cause overflow

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2022-23568: the overflow check uses a 32-bit int for a value that should be int64_t, allowing the check to be bypassed via truncation. The fix removes this faulty check entirely and replaces it with TensorShape::BuildTensorShape which properly validates dimensions. The CWE-190 (integer overflow) matches the CVE's CWE.

actual_issue_summary: The overflow check in AddManySparseToTensorsMap used an `int` (32-bit) variable `new_num_elements` to accumulate the result of `MultiplyWithoutOverflow` (which returns int64_t), causing silent truncation that could bypass the overflow detection. Additionally, `TensorShape` was constructed directly from user-provided dimensions without safe validation. The fix replaces the faulty manual overflow check with `TensorShape::BuildTensorShape`, which properly validates dimensions and returns an error status on overflow instead of crashing via CHECK-fail.

cve_desc: A Vulnerability of LG Electronic web OS TV Emulator could allow an attacker to escalate privileges and overwrite certain files. This vulnerability is due to wrong environment setting. An attacker could exploit this vulnerability through crafted configuration files and executable files.

undesired_operation.description: When argc == 9 and argv[8] equals "*", arg_start is set to 9. The subsequent access argv[arg_start][0] (i.e., argv[9][0]) reads out of bounds since valid indices are 0 through argc-1 (0..8).
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from attacker-controlled IRC server message. Could cause a crash (denial of service) or potentially read adjacent memory contents.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: argc is 9, so 9 < 8 is false; execution continues.
argc is 9, so 9 > 8 is true; we enter the block.

step_by_step_execution.description: arg_start is computed: strcmp(argv[8], "*") == 0 is true, so arg_start = 9
argv[arg_start][0] is accessed, i.e., argv[9][0]. Since argc==9, valid indices are 0..8, so argv[9] is out of bounds.

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the fix in the diff. The vulnerable code sets arg_start=9 when argc==9 and argv[8]=="*", causing an out-of-bounds access on argv[9]. The fix adds the check (argc > 9) before allowing arg_start to be set to 9. This is a crash/denial-of-service from a malformed IRC 352 message, which is exactly what the commit message describes.

actual_issue_summary: When a malformed IRC 352 (who) message has exactly 9 arguments with argv[8]=="*", arg_start is set to 9, causing an out-of-bounds array access on argv[9] (since valid indices are 0-8). The fix adds a check (argc > 9) before setting arg_start to 9.

cve_desc: NULL Pointer Dereference in Homebrew mruby prior to 3.2.

undesired_operation.description: When val is 0 (NOVAL), codegen calls do not push values onto the compile-time stack, but pop_n(len*2) and pop() calls in the NODE_KW_REST_ARGS branch still decrement the stack pointer, causing a stack pointer underflow. This generates bytecode with corrupt stack references.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack pointer underflow corrupts compiled bytecode stack references, potentially leading to out-of-bounds memory access or code execution when the generated bytecode is later executed.

initial_state.value: non-NULL, points to hash AST nodes

step_by_step_execution.description: First iteration processes a regular key-value pair, incrementing len

in_state.value: non-NULL, first node is not NODE_KW_REST_ARGS

step_by_step_execution.description: Second iteration encounters NODE_KW_REST_ARGS node

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: len is 1 from the previous regular key-value pair. The vulnerable code does not check val, so it enters this block even when val=0.

step_by_step_execution.description: pop_n(len*2) decrements stack pointer by 2, but since val=0 no values were actually pushed by codegen, causing stack pointer underflow

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2022-0481: when val is 0, the pop_n and pop operations in the NODE_KW_REST_ARGS branch execute without corresponding pushes, causing a stack pointer underflow. This is exactly what the fix addresses by adding `val &&` guards. The CVE manifests as a NULL pointer dereference (the symptom), while the finding describes the underlying cause (stack pointer corruption when val=0). The CWEs differ (CWE-476 vs CWE-191/787) but the identified bug is the same.

actual_issue_summary: In gen_hash(), when val is 0 (meaning no value is needed), the NODE_KW_REST_ARGS branch still performed pop_n() and pop() operations that assume values were pushed onto the compile-time stack. This stack pointer underflow led to a NULL pointer dereference crash. The fix adds `val &&` checks to skip these stack operations when val is 0.

cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the send_control_msg function in hw/char/virtio-serial-bus.c in QEMU before 2.4.0 allows guest users to cause a denial of service (QEMU process crash) via a crafted virtio control message.

undesired_operation.description: memcpy copies 'len' bytes into the guest-provided scatter-gather buffer elem.in_sg[0].iov_base without verifying that len <= elem.in_sg[0].iov_len, allowing a write beyond the buffer supplied by the guest.
undesired_operation.impact: A malicious guest can supply a virtqueue buffer smaller than 'len', causing a heap buffer overflow in QEMU's address space, potentially leading to guest-to-host escape or denial of service.

state.value: size of the control message being sent
guest-controlled, can be smaller than len

initial_state.value: pointer to control message data to send
unset, will be populated by virtqueue_pop
unset, will be set to vser->c_ivq

step_by_step_execution.description: vq is set to vser->c_ivq

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The queue must be ready for the function to proceed past the first check
A guest-supplied buffer element is available in the queue

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy copies len bytes from buf into elem.in_sg[0].iov_base without checking elem.in_sg[0].iov_len

in_state.value: size of control message (e.g. sizeof(struct virtio_console_control) + payload)
guest-controlled, can be smaller than len

out_state.value: len bytes written, overflowing if len > elem.in_sg[0].iov_len

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the CVE-2015-5745 vulnerability: the unchecked memcpy into a guest-supplied scatter-gather buffer that can be smaller than len, causing a buffer overflow. The fix replaces memcpy with iov_from_buf which handles scatter-gather properly.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2015-5745: In send_control_msg, memcpy writes len bytes directly into elem.in_sg[0].iov_base without checking the buffer size or handling scatter-gather lists properly. A guest can provide a buffer smaller than the control message, causing a heap buffer overflow in QEMU. The fix replaces memcpy with iov_from_buf which correctly handles iovec scatter-gather buffers.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseTensorSliceDataset` has an undefined behavior: under certain condition it can be made to dereference a `nullptr` value. The 3 input arguments to `SparseTensorSliceDataset` represent a sparse tensor. However, there are some preconditions that these arguments must satisfy but these are not validated in the implementation. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read when indices is a matrix with 0 columns (e.g. shape [N, 0]). The loop iterates dim_size(0) times and accesses column 0 of a matrix that has no columns.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure of heap contents. An attacker who controls the input tensors can craft indices with shape [N, 0] to trigger an out-of-bounds memory read.

step_by_step_execution.description: indices is read from input

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: [N, 0] is a 2D tensor so it passes the IsMatrix check

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop begins iterating over indices->dim_size(0) rows

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: N > 0 so the loop body executes

step_by_step_execution.description: Access column 0 of a matrix with 0 columns, triggering out-of-bounds read

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies a specific instance of the missing precondition validation that the CVE describes. The fix adds checks including `dense_shape->shape().dim_size(0) == indices->shape().dim_size(1)` and `dense_shape->NumElements() > 0`, which together ensure indices must have at least 1 column, preventing this exact OOB access. This is a valid component of CVE-2022-21736's undefined behavior from incomplete sparse tensor validation.

actual_issue_summary: The SparseTensorSliceDataset implementation lacked proper validation of sparse tensor preconditions: it didn't check that values count matches indices first dimension, that dense_shape dimensions match indices second dimension, or that dense_shape has at least one element. These missing checks could lead to undefined behavior including nullptr dereference and out-of-bounds access.

cve_desc: An out-of-bounds read vulnerability was discovered in the PCRE2 library in the compile_xclass_matchingpath() function of the pcre2_jit_compile.c file. This involves a unicode property matching issue in JIT-compiled regular expressions. The issue occurs because the character was not fully read in case-less matching within JIT.

undesired_operation.description: In the PT_CLIST code generation branch for `is_powerof2(other_cases[2] ^ other_cases[1])` with nonzero charoffset, the wrong XOR mask `other_cases[1] ^ other_cases[0]` is used instead of the correct `other_cases[2] ^ other_cases[1]`, generating incorrect JIT matching code that matches wrong characters.
code_snippets: OP2(SLJIT_OR, TMP2, 0, TMP2, 0, SLJIT_IMM, other_cases[1] ^ other_cases[0]);
undesired_operation.impact: Incorrect regex matching in JIT-compiled code. When PCRE2 regexes are used for input validation or security filtering, the generated code may incorrectly match or fail to match characters in caseless sets, potentially allowing bypass of regex-based security controls.

step_by_step_execution.description: Code enters the is_powerof2(other_cases[2] ^ other_cases[1]) branch with nonzero charoffset
Wrong XOR mask applied: other_cases[1] ^ other_cases[0] instead of other_cases[2] ^ other_cases[1]

judgments.reasoning: This finding identifies a separate pre-existing code issue (wrong XOR mask in a specific PT_CLIST optimization branch) that is unrelated to the CVE-2022-1586 fix. The CVE fix is specifically the `*cc == XCL_PROP` → `cc[-1] == XCL_PROP` change in the scanning/min-max calculation phase. This wrong XOR mask exists in both the vulnerable and fixed versions and is not part of the CVE.

undesired_operation.description: The condition `*cc == PT_CLIST && *cc == XCL_PROP` is always false (since PT_CLIST != XCL_PROP), preventing proper min/max range calculation for PT_CLIST property entries. The intended check was likely `*cc == PT_CLIST && cc[-1] == XCL_PROP`.
undesired_operation.impact: The caseless set characters are never used to narrow the min/max range during the scanning phase, always falling through to the else branch that sets max=READ_CHAR_MAX and min=0. While this causes suboptimal code generation (overly broad character reading), in 32-bit non-UTF mode it may cause incorrect behavior since the range check affects how characters are validated.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: PT_CLIST and XCL_PROP are different constants, so *cc cannot equal both simultaneously. The condition is always false regardless of input.

step_by_step_execution.description: Falls through to else branch, setting max=READ_CHAR_MAX, min=0 instead of computing proper min/max from caseless sets

judgments.reasoning: This finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed in CVE-2022-1586. The condition `*cc == PT_CLIST && *cc == XCL_PROP` is always false because it checks `*cc` against two different constants. The fix changes `*cc == XCL_PROP` to `cc[-1] == XCL_PROP`. This causes incorrect value reading in JIT caseless matching, which is precisely the out-of-bounds read vulnerability described in the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The bug was a typo in the condition `*cc == PT_CLIST && *cc == XCL_PROP` where the second `*cc` should have been `cc[-1]`. Since PT_CLIST != XCL_PROP, this condition was always false, preventing proper min/max character range computation for caseless sets in JIT-compiled regexes. This caused the character to not be fully read in case-less matching within JIT, leading to an out-of-bounds read.

cve_desc: In Deark before v1.5.8, a specially crafted input file can cause a division by zero in (src/fmtutil.c) because of the value of pixelsize.

undesired_operation.description: Division by zero when bi->pixelsize is 0, as pixelsize is read from untrusted input and used as a divisor without validation.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (SIGFPE / floating point exception on division by zero)

initial_state.value: unset (will be read from input file)

step_by_step_execution.description: bi->pixelsize is read from untrusted input at pos+18
Division (bi->rowbytes*8)/bi->pixelsize is executed with pixelsize=0

out_state.value: undefined — division by zero triggers crash

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-28856: a division by zero in src/fmtutil.c due to the value of pixelsize being 0. The CWE, code location, and impact all align with the CVE description and the fix.

actual_issue_summary: In fmtutil_macbitmap_read_pixmap_only_fields(), bi->pixelsize is read from the input file and used as a divisor without checking for zero. A crafted file with pixelsize=0 causes division by zero. The fix adds a guard (if(bi->pixelsize>0)) before performing the division.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `SparseCountSparseOutput` is vulnerable to a heap overflow. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: per_batch_counts is accessed with a negative index when batch (derived from attacker-controlled indices tensor) is negative. The code only checks `batch >= num_batches` but never checks `batch < 0`, allowing out-of-bounds memory access on the vector.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read/write on the per_batch_counts vector. An attacker providing a negative value in the indices tensor's first column can corrupt memory or crash the process, potentially leading to code execution.

step_by_step_execution.description: shape tensor is read, is_1d is false since shape has 2 elements, num_batches = shape_vector(0) = 1
per_batch_counts is allocated with num_batches=1
In the loop, batch is read from indices_values(0, 0) which is -1

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: batch=-1 is not >= num_batches=1, so the upper-bound check passes and no error is raised

step_by_step_execution.description: per_batch_counts[batch][value] is accessed with batch=-1, causing heap overflow/out-of-bounds write

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE. The advisory shows the PoC uses indices=[[-1, -1]], and the vulnerability is that negative batch indices bypass the `batch >= num_batches` check, leading to heap overflow via out-of-bounds access on per_batch_counts. This is the heap overflow described in CVE-2022-21740.

actual_issue_summary: The SparseCountSparseOutput implementation allows negative indices (e.g., indices=[[-1,-1]]) to be used as batch indices into per_batch_counts vector. The code only validates `batch >= num_batches` (upper bound) but not `batch < 0` (lower bound), allowing heap overflow via negative vector indexing. The fix restructures the function to use splits-based batching instead of index-based batching, eliminating the attack vector.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. When a user does not supply arguments that determine a valid sparse tensor, `tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset` implementation can be made to dereference a null pointer. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L240-L251) has some argument validation but fails to consider the case when either `indices` or `values` are provided for an empty sparse tensor when the other is not. If `indices` is empty, then [code that performs validation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/data/sparse_tensor_slice_dataset_op.cc#L260-L261) (i.e., checking that the indices are monotonically increasing) results in a null pointer dereference. If `indices` as provided by the user is empty, then `indices` in the C++ code above is backed by an empty `std::vector`, hence calling `indices->dim_size(0)` results in null pointer dereferencing (same as calling `std::vector::at()` on an empty vector). We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 02cc160e29d20631de3859c6653184e3f876b9d7. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When indices has shape [N, 0] (a valid matrix with 0 columns but N > 0 rows), the loop accesses indices->matrix<int64>()(i, 0) which reads column 0 from a matrix with 0 columns, causing an out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure of heap contents. An attacker providing a crafted SparseTensor with indices of shape [N, 0] can trigger the out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: indices shape is checked to be a matrix

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: dim_size(0) = N > 0, so loop executes

step_by_step_execution.description: Access indices->matrix<int64>()(i, 0) on a matrix with 0 columns

out_state.value: out-of-bounds read from column 0 of a 0-column matrix

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2021-37647) is specifically about a null pointer dereference when either indices or values is empty but the other is not. The fix adds a check ensuring that if one of indices/values is empty, the other must also be empty. The finding describes a different hypothetical issue (OOB read with shape [N, 0]), which is not the vulnerability addressed by the CVE. The actual CVE concerns the case where indices is completely empty (e.g., shape [0, K]) and values is non-empty, leading to a null pointer dereference in SparseTensor::Create or similar downstream code.

actual_issue_summary: Null pointer dereference in tf.raw_ops.SparseTensorSliceDataset when either indices or values is empty but the other is not. The fix adds validation that if either indices or values has 0 elements, the other must also have 0 elements.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `SparseFillEmptyRows` can be made to trigger a heap OOB access. This occurs whenever the size of `indices` does not match the size of `values`. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -30,6 +30,12 @@
                                             values_t.shape().DebugString()),
                     done);
   OP_REQUIRES_ASYNC(
+      context, indices_t.dim_size(0) == values_t.dim_size(0),
+      errors::InvalidArgument("The length of `values` (", values_t.dim_size(0),
+                              ") must match the first dimension of `indices` (",
+                              indices_t.dim_size(0), ")."),
+      done);
+  OP_REQUIRES_ASYNC(
       context, TensorShapeUtils::IsScalar(default_value_t.shape()),
       errors::InvalidArgument("default_value must be a scalar, saw: ",
                               default_value_t.shape().DebugString()),

undesired_operation.description: The function does not validate that indices_t.dim_size(0) equals values_t.dim_size(0), nor that indices_t.dim_size(1) equals dense_shape_t.NumElements(). This allows mismatched tensors to be passed to the SparseFillEmptyRows functor, which will perform out-of-bounds reads/writes when iterating over indices using the values count or vice versa.
code_snippets:
OP_REQUIRES_OK_ASYNC(context,
                       FunctorType()(context, default_value_t, indices_t,
                                     values_t, dense_shape_t, done),
                       done);
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (heap out-of-bounds read/write) or potential information disclosure when the functor iterates over indices/values with mismatched dimensions

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing validation that indices_t.dim_size(0) must equal values_t.dim_size(0). This is exactly the vulnerability described in CVE-2021-41224, where mismatched sizes of indices and values triggers a heap OOB access in SparseFillEmptyRows. The fix adds precisely this check.

actual_issue_summary: SparseFillEmptyRows did not validate that the first dimension of `indices` matches the length of `values`. When these sizes differ, the functor performs heap out-of-bounds reads. The fix adds an OP_REQUIRES_ASYNC check that indices_t.dim_size(0) == values_t.dim_size(0).

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can read from outside of bounds of heap allocated data by sending specially crafted illegal arguments to `tf.raw_ops.SdcaOptimizerV2`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/sdca_internal.cc#L320-L353) does not check that the length of `example_labels` is the same as the number of examples. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit a4e138660270e7599793fa438cd7b2fc2ce215a6. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: example_labels is accessed with indices derived from example_weights.size() (num_examples), but there is no check that example_labels has at least num_examples elements. If the example_labels tensor is smaller than example_weights, the access example_labels(example_id) reads out of bounds.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read — attacker can craft a TensorFlow graph where example_labels has fewer elements than example_weights, causing a heap buffer over-read that can leak memory contents or crash the process (denial of service).

state.value: 0 to num_examples-1, can exceed example_labels bounds

example_labels tensor is read from context and flattened

out_state.value: flat<float> from input tensor, e.g. size=2

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-37672: the missing validation that example_labels length equals num_examples allows an out-of-bounds heap read. The fix adds precisely this check.

actual_issue_summary: The function Examples::Initialize does not validate that example_labels has the same number of elements as example_weights (num_examples). An attacker can provide a smaller example_labels tensor, causing out-of-bounds heap reads when iterating with indices derived from num_examples.

cve_desc: Memory leak in the afra_box_read function in MP4Box in GPAC 1.0.1 allows attackers to read memory via a crafted file.

undesired_operation.description: When ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE fails inside the loops after gf_malloc has allocated a GF_AfraEntry or GF_GlobalAfraEntry, the allocated memory is never freed and never inserted into any list, causing a memory leak.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service through memory exhaustion when repeatedly parsing crafted files that trigger the error path, leaking one struct allocation per malformed box.

state.value: freshly allocated via gf_malloc, not yet inserted into any list
fails due to crafted file with insufficient remaining size

initial_state.value: remaining box size after initial 9-byte decrease
read from file, could be nonzero

step_by_step_execution.description: ae is allocated via gf_malloc

out_state.value: valid heap pointer to GF_AfraEntry or GF_GlobalAfraEntry

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: malloc succeeded

step_by_step_execution.description: ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE is called, which checks if ptr->size >= required bytes. In a crafted file, ptr->size is too small, so the macro returns an error.

in_state.value: valid heap pointer, not inserted into any list

out_state.value: leaked - pointer lost on function return

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the memory leak in afra_box_read. The CVE describes a memory leak in this function, and the fix moves gf_list_insert before ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE so that allocated entries are tracked in lists before any error return, preventing the leak. The finding accurately describes this issue.

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in afra_box_read where allocated GF_AfraEntry and GF_GlobalAfraEntry structs are inserted into their respective lists only after ISOM_DECREASE_SIZE checks. If those checks fail, the allocated memory is leaked. The fix moves gf_list_insert calls to immediately after allocation, before any error-returning macros.

cve_desc: This affects the package drogonframework/drogon before 1.7.5. The unsafe handling of file names during upload using HttpFile::save() method may enable attackers to write files to arbitrary locations outside the designated target folder.

undesired_operation.description: The member fileName_ (originating from an HTTP multipart upload's filename field) is appended to the save path without sanitization. An attacker can supply a filename like '../../etc/cron.d/evil' to write the uploaded file to an arbitrary location on the filesystem.
undesired_operation.impact: Arbitrary file write — attacker can place files at any writable path on the server, potentially achieving remote code execution (e.g., writing a cron job, overwriting executables, or overwriting configuration).

state.value: attacker-controlled string containing path traversal sequences like ../../etc/cron.d/evil

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled filename from HTTP multipart upload, e.g. '../../etc/cron.d/evil'

step_by_step_execution.description: fsPath is constructed from the path parameter

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: 'uploads' is not absolute, has no parent path component starting with . or .., so it gets prefixed with upload path

step_by_step_execution.description: fsPath is prepended with the configured upload path
fsFileName is constructed from the attacker-controlled fileName_ member without any sanitization
The final save path is computed as fsPath / fsFileName, which resolves the traversal

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: the unsanitized fileName_ from HTTP upload is concatenated to the save path, allowing path traversal to write files outside the intended upload directory. This matches CVE-2022-25297 exactly. The CWE-22 (Path Traversal) is closely related to the official CWE-552 (Files Accessible to External Parties) — both describe the same underlying issue of unsafe file path handling.

actual_issue_summary: The HttpFile::save() method used the attacker-controlled fileName_ member (from HTTP multipart upload filename field) unsanitized when constructing the final file path via `fsPath / fsFileName`. An attacker could craft a filename with path traversal sequences (e.g., '../../') to write uploaded files to arbitrary locations outside the intended upload directory. The fix removes the use of the internal fileName_ in the save path construction, instead having the caller explicitly provide the full target filename.

cve_desc: The kernel in Amazon Web Services FreeRTOS before 10.4.3 has an integer overflow in queue.c for queue creation.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in uxQueueLength * uxItemSize results in a small xQueueSizeInBytes. The subsequent pvPortMalloc allocates a too-small buffer, but the queue is initialized to hold uxQueueLength items of uxItemSize bytes each, leading to heap buffer overflow when items are enqueued.
code_snippets: pxNewQueue = ( Queue_t * ) pvPortMalloc( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes );
prvInitialiseNewQueue( uxQueueLength, uxItemSize, pucQueueStorage, ucQueueType, pxNewQueue );
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — when items are later enqueued into the queue, writes will exceed the allocated buffer, potentially leading to code execution or corruption of heap metadata.

state.value: wraps to a small value due to multiplication overflow of uxQueueLength * uxItemSize

step_by_step_execution.description: xQueueSizeInBytes is computed as uxQueueLength * uxItemSize, which overflows
pvPortMalloc allocates sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes bytes (too small)

judgments.reasoning: The finding describes the multiplication overflow (uxQueueLength * uxItemSize), but the vulnerable code already contains a configASSERT check for that multiplication overflow. The actual CVE (CVE-2021-31571) and its fix address the missing addition overflow check: sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes can wrap around, causing pvPortMalloc to allocate a too-small buffer. The finding identifies the wrong integer overflow.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability is a missing integer overflow check on the addition of sizeof(Queue_t) + xQueueSizeInBytes in xQueueGenericCreate(). While the multiplication overflow (uxQueueLength * uxItemSize) was already checked, the subsequent addition with sizeof(Queue_t) could wrap around, causing pvPortMalloc to allocate a too-small buffer. The fix adds configASSERT( ( sizeof( Queue_t ) + xQueueSizeInBytes ) > xQueueSizeInBytes ) to detect this addition overflow.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

undesired_operation.description: The while loop condition checks VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1)) BEFORE checking s > 0. When s reaches 0, this reads *(bd->textstart - 1), an out-of-bounds read. If that OOB byte is whitespace, the loop continues with s going negative, decrementing pnew past the buffer start, and *pnew = NUL writes before the allocated heap buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap read leading to potential heap buffer underwrite (NUL byte written before allocated buffer), which can cause heap corruption, crash, or potentially arbitrary code execution.

state.value: 0 or negative due to missing early bounds check
decremented past the start of the allocated buffer

initial_state.value: length of selected text block (could be 0 for empty/whitespace-only lines)
pointer to start of text in the line

step_by_step_execution.description: Buffer is allocated and pnew is set to point to it

out_state.value: points to start of allocated heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: pnew is advanced past startspaces, textlen, and endspaces

in_state.value: points to start of allocated buffer

out_state.value: points to end of written data in buffer

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The caller passes exclude_trailing_space as true to trigger trailing space removal

step_by_step_execution.description: s is initialized to bd->textlen + bd->endspaces. With endspaces=0 and a small or all-whitespace textlen, s starts small.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: When s=1, bd->textstart+0 is the first character which is whitespace, so VIM_ISWHITE is true and s>0 is true

step_by_step_execution.description: s is decremented: s = s - mb_head_off(...) - 1. For a single-byte whitespace char, mb_head_off returns 0, so s becomes 0. pnew is also decremented.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With s=0, the VIM_ISWHITE check is evaluated FIRST (before s>0 check). It reads *(bd->textstart - 1) which is an out-of-bounds read. If that byte happens to be whitespace (0x20 or 0x09), the condition is true and the loop body executes again.

relevant_state.value: out-of-bounds memory, could contain whitespace byte

step_by_step_execution.description: s is decremented further to a negative value, pnew is decremented past the buffer start

out_state.value: points before the allocated heap buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: *pnew = NUL writes a NUL byte before the allocated heap buffer

in_state.value: points before the allocated heap buffer

out_state.value: NUL written to heap memory before the allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: the while loop condition evaluates VIM_ISWHITE before checking s > 0, leading to an out-of-bounds read when s reaches 0 and potential heap buffer underwrite via the *pnew = NUL write. This matches the CVE-2022-0407 fix which reorders the conditions to check s > 0 first. The CWEs (CWE-125 for OOB read, CWE-787 for heap-based buffer overflow/underwrite) are both applicable.

actual_issue_summary: In yank_copy_line(), the while loop condition checked VIM_ISWHITE(*(bd->textstart + s - 1)) before checking s > 0. When s reaches 0, this reads memory before bd->textstart (out-of-bounds). If the loop continues, pnew can be decremented past the buffer start, and *pnew = NUL writes before the allocated heap buffer. The fix reorders the conditions to check s > 0 first (short-circuit evaluation prevents the OOB access).

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in MKL implementation of requantization, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantization_range_per_channel_op.cc) does not validate the dimensions of the `input` tensor. A similar issue occurs in `MklRequantizePerChannelOp`. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/core/kernels/mkl/mkl_requantize_per_channel_op.cc) does not perform full validation for all the input arguments. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 9e62869465573cb2d9b5053f1fa02a81fce21d69 and in the Github commit 203214568f5bc237603dbab6e1fd389f1572f5c9. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: transposed_input.chip<0>(i) accesses row i of the transposed input matrix, but the loop bound 'depth' is derived from input_max.NumElements() which is never validated against the actual inner dimension of the 'input' tensor. If depth exceeds the inner dimension, chip<0>(i) performs an out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read on the input tensor data, leading to information disclosure of adjacent memory or a crash (denial of service). An attacker can craft TensorFlow graph inputs where input_max has more elements than the last dimension of the input tensor.

state.value: derived from input_max.NumElements(), potentially larger than input's inner dimension
0 to depth-1, may exceed valid range for transposed_input

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2021-37665: the vulnerable code does not validate the input tensor's dimensions against depth (derived from input_max), allowing out-of-bounds access. The fix adds exactly these missing checks (input.dims()==4, input.dim_size(3)==depth, input.NumElements()>0).

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2021-37665 is caused by incomplete validation in MklRequantizationRangePerChannel. The code derives 'depth' from input_max.NumElements() but never validates that the input tensor is non-empty, is 4-dimensional, or that its channel dimension matches depth. This allows null pointer dereference (empty input) or out-of-bounds memory access. The fix adds checks for input.NumElements()>0, input.dims()==4, input.dim_size(3)==depth, and input_min.NumElements()==depth.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions due to incomplete validation in `tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2`, an attacker can trigger undefined behavior via binding a reference to a null pointer or can access data outside the bounds of heap allocated arrays. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/84d053187cb80d975ef2b9684d4b61981bca0c41/tensorflow/core/kernels/quantize_op.cc#L59) has some validation but does not check that `min_range` and `max_range` both have the same non-zero number of elements. If `axis` is provided (i.e., not `-1`), then validation should check that it is a value in range for the rank of `input` tensor and then the lengths of `min_range` and `max_range` inputs match the `axis` dimension of the `input` tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 6da6620efad397c85493b8f8667b821403516708. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -5,7 +5,50 @@
 
     int num_slices = 1;
     if (axis_ > -1) {
+      OP_REQUIRES(
+          ctx, input.dims() > axis_,
+          errors::InvalidArgument(
+              "Axis is on a zero-based index, so its value must always be less "
+              "than number of input's dims, but given axis value was ",
+              axis_, " and input's dims was ", input.dims()));
       num_slices = input.dim_size(axis_);
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_min_range.dims() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, min_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but min_range dims are ",
+                      input_min_range.dims()));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_min_range.dim_size(0) == num_slices,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, min_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but min_range is a 1-D tensor of size ",
+                      input_min_range.dim_size(0),
+                      " and input's axis dimension is of size ", num_slices));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_max_range.dims() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, max_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but max_range dims are ",
+                      input_max_range.dims()));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_max_range.dim_size(0) == num_slices,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is specified, max_range must be a 1-D tensor "
+                      "whose size matches the axis dimension of the input and "
+                      "output tensors, but max_range is a 1-D tensor of size ",
+                      input_max_range.dim_size(0),
+                      " and input's axis dimension is of size ", num_slices));
+    } else {
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_min_range.NumElements() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is not specified, min_range must contain a "
+                      "single float element, but it contains ",
+                      input_min_range.NumElements(), " elements"));
+      OP_REQUIRES(ctx, input_max_range.NumElements() == 1,
+                  errors::InvalidArgument(
+                      "If axis is not specified, max_range must contain a "
+                      "single float element, but it contains ",
+                      input_max_range.NumElements(), " elements"));
     }
 
     const TensorShape& minmax_shape = ctx->input(1).shape();

Loop iterates num_slices (10) times, accessing min_ranges(i) and max_ranges(i) which only have 2 elements, causing OOB read at i >= 2

The CVE describes incomplete validation in QuantizeV2 where min_range and max_range sizes are not checked to match the axis dimension of the input tensor, leading to OOB access. The fix adds exactly these validations. The finding correctly identifies the OOB read in the loop over num_slices.

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation in tf.raw_ops.QuantizeV2: when axis is specified, there is no check that (1) axis is within bounds of input tensor rank, (2) min_range and max_range are 1-D tensors whose sizes match input.dim_size(axis). When axis is -1, there is no check that min_range and max_range each contain exactly one element. This allows null pointer dereference and out-of-bounds heap access.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.StagePeek` does not fully validate the input arguments. This results in a `CHECK`-failure which can be used to trigger a denial of service attack. The code assumes `index` is a scalar but there is no validation for this before accessing its value. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.

undesired_operation.description: A negative int value from user input is implicitly cast to std::size_t (unsigned), producing a very large index value that is passed to buf->Peek(), potentially causing an out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds memory read leading to information disclosure or denial of service (crash). An attacker supplies a negative integer as the operation's input tensor, which wraps to a huge size_t index.

state.value: very large value due to negative int cast to size_t

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled tensor with negative scalar int value

step_by_step_execution.description: The scalar int value is read from the input tensor and cast to std::size_t
buf->Peek is called with the wrapped large index

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2022-29195) is specifically about the missing validation that the input tensor is a scalar. When a non-scalar tensor is passed, calling `.scalar<int>()()` triggers a CHECK-failure causing a denial of service crash. The fix adds `TensorShapeUtils::IsScalar(ctx->input(0).shape())` validation. The finding instead describes a signed-to-unsigned integer conversion issue (negative int to size_t), which is a different problem from the one the CVE addresses. The actual vulnerability is about tensor shape, not about the sign of the value.

actual_issue_summary: The implementation of tf.raw_ops.StagePeek does not validate that the `index` input is a scalar tensor before calling `.scalar<int>()()`. Passing a non-scalar tensor triggers an internal CHECK-failure, causing a denial of service crash. The fix adds a shape validation check (`TensorShapeUtils::IsScalar`) before accessing the scalar value.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.3 was discovered to contain a stack overflow in the function njs_default_module_loader at /src/njs/src/njs_module.c. NOTE: multiple third parties dispute this report, e.g., the behavior is only found in unreleased development code that was not part of the 0.7.2, 0.7.3, or 0.7.4 release

undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to potential arbitrary code execution. An attacker who controls the module name and directory can overwrite the return address or other stack data.

This matches the diff fix and the CVE description of a stack overflow in njs_module.c.

actual_issue_summary: In njs_module_path(), when dir is not NULL, `length = dir->length` overwrites the previously stored info->name.length instead of adding to it (`length += dir->length`). This means the subsequent bounds check against NJS_MAX_PATH only validates dir->length, not the total of dir->length + info->name.length, allowing a stack buffer overflow in the src[] array.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. A buffer overflow vulnerability in versions 2.12 and prior affects applications that use PJSIP DNS resolution. It doesn't affect PJSIP users who utilize an external resolver. This vulnerability is related to CVE-2023-27585. The difference is that this issue is in parsing the query record `parse_rr()`, while the issue in CVE-2023-27585 is in `parse_query()`. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. A workaround is to disable DNS resolution in PJSIP config (by setting `nameserver_count` to zero) or use an external resolver instead.

undesired_operation.description: pj_memcpy reads 2 bytes for query type and 2 bytes for query class from pointer p (start + name_part_len) without verifying that p+4 <= max, causing an out-of-bounds read
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of heap/stack memory beyond packet buffer, or crash (denial of service) when reading unmapped memory

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies exactly the issue fixed by the commit: missing bounds check (p + 4 > max) before reading type and class fields via pj_memcpy in parse_query(). While the CVE description primarily references parse_rr(), the commit also fixes parse_query() as part of the same vulnerability class, and the code shown is parse_query(). The CWE differs (CWE-125 vs CWE-787) but the underlying bug — missing bounds check leading to out-of-bounds memory access — is the same. The finding correctly matches the fix in the diff.

actual_issue_summary: Buffer overflow in PJSIP DNS packet parsing due to missing bounds checks. In parse_query(), after parsing the name, the code reads 4 bytes (type and class fields) via pj_memcpy without checking that p+4 doesn't exceed the packet buffer boundary (max). The fix adds `if (p + 4 > max) return PJLIB_UTIL_EDNSINSIZE;` before the reads. Additional fixes were applied to get_name_len() and get_name() for label length validation.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the shape inference function for `Transpose` is vulnerable to a heap buffer overflow. This occurs whenever `perm` contains negative elements. The shape inference function does not validate that the indices in `perm` are all valid. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: Negative permutation index is not checked before being passed to c->Dim(input, in_idx). The code only validates in_idx >= rank but never checks in_idx < 0, allowing a negative index to be used for an out-of-bounds read.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read via negative index passed to Dim(), potentially leading to information disclosure or crash (denial of service).

initial_state.value: determined from input rank or perm elements

step_by_step_execution.description: perm tensor is read and converted to int64 data vector

out_state.value: vector of int64 values including negative values

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates; in_idx is read from data[i]

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: in_idx is negative (e.g. -5), which is less than rank (positive), so the bounds check passes and does NOT return an error

step_by_step_execution.description: c->Dim(input, in_idx) is called with negative in_idx, causing heap buffer overflow / out-of-bounds access

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2021-41216: missing validation for negative perm indices leads to out-of-bounds access in the Transpose shape inference function. The fix adds the check `in_idx <= -rank` which is exactly what the finding describes as missing.

actual_issue_summary: The Transpose shape inference function only checked if perm indices were >= rank but did not check for negative values below -rank, allowing negative indices to cause a heap buffer overflow/out-of-bounds read when passed to c->Dim(). The fix adds the check `in_idx <= -rank` to reject such values.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of SVDF in TFLite is [vulnerable to a null pointer error](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/svdf.cc#L300-L313). The [`GetVariableInput` function](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L115-L119) can return a null pointer but `GetTensorData` assumes that the argument is always a valid tensor. Furthermore, because `GetVariableInput` calls [`GetMutableInput`](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/460e000de3a83278fb00b61a16d161b1964f15f4/tensorflow/lite/kernels/kernel_util.cc#L82-L90) which might return `nullptr`, the `tensor->is_variable` expression can also trigger a null pointer exception. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 5b048e87e4e55990dae6b547add4dae59f4e1c76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: tensor pointer is dereferenced (tensor->is_variable) without a null check after GetMutableInput, which can return nullptr. This causes a null pointer dereference.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash. An attacker who can influence the node/index such that GetMutableInput returns nullptr triggers a null pointer dereference.

step_by_step_execution.description: GetMutableInput is called and returns nullptr

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: There is no null check in the vulnerable code, so execution proceeds directly to the dereference

step_by_step_execution.description: tensor->is_variable is accessed, dereferencing a null pointer

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2021-37681: the missing null check on the return value of GetMutableInput before dereferencing tensor->is_variable. The fix adds exactly the null check described. CWE-476 (NULL Pointer Dereference) is consistent with the CVE's CWE-369, as the NVD description explicitly states this is a null pointer error.

actual_issue_summary: GetVariableInput dereferences the tensor pointer returned by GetMutableInput (via tensor->is_variable) without first checking if it is nullptr. GetMutableInput can return nullptr, causing a null pointer dereference. The fix adds a null check before the dereference.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library written in C. PJSIP versions 2.12 and prior do not parse incoming RTCP feedback RPSI (Reference Picture Selection Indication) packet, but any app that directly uses pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() will be affected. A patch is available in the `master` branch of the `pjsip/pjproject` GitHub repository. There are currently no known workarounds.

undesired_operation.description: Unsigned integer underflow in rpsi_bit_len calculation when attacker-controlled padlen exceeds rpsi_len*8 - 16, causing pj_strset to record an enormous string length, leading to out-of-bounds access by any consumer of rpsi->rpsi.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure or crash — an attacker sends a crafted RTCP packet with a large padlen value, causing the returned rpsi structure to reference a string with an enormous length, leading to out-of-bounds read when the caller processes it.

The fix adds checks for padlen >= 32 and rpsi_len*8 < 16+padlen, exactly addressing this issue.

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation of header length and padlen in pjmedia_rtcp_fb_parse_rpsi() allows a crafted RTCP RPSI packet to cause an unsigned integer underflow in rpsi_bit_len (when padlen > rpsi_len*8 - 16), resulting in out-of-bounds read/write. The fix adds checks for minimum header length, padlen < 32, and rpsi_len*8 >= 16+padlen.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions an attacker can trigger undefined behavior, integer overflows, segfaults and `CHECK`-fail crashes if they can change saved checkpoints from outside of TensorFlow. This is because the checkpoints loading infrastructure is missing validation for invalid file formats. The fixes will be included in TensorFlow 2.7.0. We will also cherrypick these commits on TensorFlow 2.6.1, TensorFlow 2.5.2, and TensorFlow 2.4.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.impact: Use-after-free can lead to crash (denial of service), information disclosure of heap contents, or potentially arbitrary code execution if an attacker can influence heap layout.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE is about missing validation of tensor data size before calling CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice, which allows buffer overflow when loading malicious checkpoints with insufficient data. The fix adds a check that the proto data size matches the expected number of elements. The finding describes a speculative race condition / use-after-free involving mutex scope, which is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Missing validation of tensor data size when loading checkpoints. CopyDataFromTensorSliceToTensorSlice was called without checking that the proto data contained the expected number of elements, allowing a buffer overflow (read out of bounds) when a malicious checkpoint provides insufficient data. The fix adds a size check comparing TensorProtoDataSize against the expected num_elements before copying.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of sparse reduction operations in TensorFlow can trigger accesses outside of bounds of heap allocated data. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/a1bc56203f21a5a4995311825ffaba7a670d7747/tensorflow/core/kernels/sparse_reduce_op.cc#L217-L228) fails to validate that each reduction group does not overflow and that each corresponding index does not point to outside the bounds of the input tensor. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 87158f43f05f2720a374f3e6d22a7aaa3a33f750. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: The flat index `idx` computed by CoordinatesToFlatIndex from sparse tensor group coordinates is used to index into `out_flat` without any bounds check. An attacker can supply sparse tensor indices that are out-of-range (negative or exceeding the declared shape), causing an out-of-bounds write via `out_flat(idx)`.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds memory read/write. An attacker providing crafted sparse tensor indices can write attacker-influenced values to arbitrary memory offsets relative to the output tensor buffer, potentially achieving code execution or causing a crash.

Sparse tensor is reordered and grouped. Groups yield coordinates from the sparse indices.

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE. The fix adds two checks: (1) that group size matches output_strides size, and (2) that idx is within [0, out_flat.size()). The finding identifies the missing bounds check on idx before the out_flat(idx) write, which is precisely the vulnerability described in CVE-2021-37635.

actual_issue_summary: In sparse reduction operations, the computed flat index from group coordinates was used to write into the output tensor without bounds checking. Crafted sparse tensor indices could produce an out-of-bounds index, leading to heap OOB read/write. The fix adds validation that the group size matches output strides and that the computed index is within the output tensor bounds.

cve_desc: OwnTone (aka owntone-server) through 28.1 has a use-after-free in net_bind() in misc.c.

undesired_operation.description: After freeaddrinfo(servinfo), the code dereferences ptr (which points into the now-freed servinfo linked list) to access ptr->ai_addr and ptr->ai_addrlen in getsockname() and subsequent calls. This is a use-after-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Use-after-free can lead to crash (denial of service), information disclosure of heap contents, or potentially arbitrary code execution if the freed memory is reallocated and attacker-controlled data is placed there.

state.value: points into freed servinfo linked list

out_state.value: points to a node within servinfo linked list

in_state.value: valid pointer to addrinfo linked list
points to a node within servinfo linked list
