judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the off-by-one buffer overrun in the cookedprint function. When atoascii fills bv to capacity and output_raw is not '*', the code appends a character and null terminator past the buffer boundary. This matches the CVE-2015-7852 vulnerability (TALOS-CAN-0063) which is a buffer overrun in ntpq's cookedprint triggered by crafted mode 6 response packets. The fix reduces sizeof(bv) by 1 in the atoascii call to reserve space for the appended character.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one stack buffer overrun in ntpq's cookedprint function: when atoascii fills bv to its full capacity and output_raw is not '*', appending the output_raw character and null terminator writes one byte past the end of the bv buffer. The fix reserves one extra byte by passing sizeof(bv)-1 to atoascii when appending will occur.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in disable_priv_mode in shell.c in GNU Bash through 5.0 patch 11. By default, if Bash is run with its effective UID not equal to its real UID, it will drop privileges by setting its effective UID to its real UID. However, it does so incorrectly. On Linux and other systems that support "saved UID" functionality, the saved UID is not dropped. An attacker with command execution in the shell can use "enable -f" for runtime loading of a new builtin, which can be a shared object that calls setuid() and therefore regains privileges. However, binaries running with an effective UID of 0 are unaffected.

undesired_operation.description: When setuid() fails (returns < 0), execution continues and current_user.euid is unconditionally set to current_user.uid, causing the program to believe privileges were dropped when they were not.
undesired_operation.impact: Privilege management bypass — the program believes it is running as an unprivileged user and may skip subsequent security checks, while the process actually retains elevated (root) privileges.

state.value: set to current_user.uid regardless of setuid() return value

initial_state.value: effective UID of the process (different from real UID)

step_by_step_execution.description: setuid(current_user.uid) is called and fails, returning < 0

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: setuid failed

step_by_step_execution.description: Error is logged but execution continues; current_user.euid set to current_user.uid

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2019-18276) is about setuid() succeeding but not dropping the saved UID on Linux systems that support saved UIDs. This allows an attacker to regain privileges by calling setuid() from a loaded shared object. The fix replaces setuid()/setgid() with setresuid()/setresgid() which also reset the saved UID. The finding describes a different, secondary issue about setuid() failure handling and stale bookkeeping, which is not the vulnerability addressed by the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: When Bash drops privileges via setuid(), the saved UID is not reset on systems supporting saved UIDs. This allows an attacker to regain elevated privileges by calling setuid() (e.g., from a runtime-loaded builtin shared object). The fix uses setresuid()/setresgid() to also reset the saved UID/GID.

cve_desc: Use-after-free vulnerability in dns.c in Tor before 0.2.2.38 might allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (daemon crash) via vectors related to failed DNS requests.

undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read that could leak heap memory contents or cause a crash (denial of service). An attacker can trigger this by sending a network-status document with exactly 2 args on the network-status-version line where the second arg is an unrecognized flavor name.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the exact bug fixed in the diff: accessing tok->args[2] when tok->n_args is 2 (meaning only indices 0 and 1 are valid). The fix changes tok->args[2] to tok->args[1]. Note: While the provided CVE ID (CVE-2012-3517) refers to a dns.c use-after-free issue, the actual commit fixes what is CVE-2012-3518 (out-of-bounds read in networkstatus_parse_vote_from_string due to bad flavor name). The finding correctly identifies the vulnerability in the provided code and diff.

actual_issue_summary: The commit fixes an out-of-bounds array access in networkstatus_parse_vote_from_string where tok->args[2] was referenced instead of tok->args[1] in an error message when parsing a bad flavor name. When n_args==2, only indices 0 and 1 are valid, so accessing index 2 causes an out-of-bounds read. This actually corresponds to CVE-2012-3518, not CVE-2012-3517 (which is a dns.c use-after-free). Both were fixed in Tor 0.2.2.38.

cve_desc: xmlparse.c in Expat (aka libexpat) before 2.4.5 allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via memory exhaustion — an attacker who can repeatedly trigger this path leaks sizeof(BINDING) bytes each time the function is called with a sufficiently long URI.

The CVE is about the lack of validation that namespace separator characters can be inserted into namespace URIs. The fix adds a check to reject URIs containing the namespace separator character. The memory leak finding is a different, unrelated issue.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerability allows attackers to insert namespace-separator characters into namespace URIs in XML namespace declarations. The fix adds a check in the addBinding function's URI iteration loop that rejects any URI containing the parser's namespace separator character, returning XML_ERROR_SYNTAX.

cve_desc: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command in bookmarking function of Newsbeuter versions 0.7 through 2.9 allows remote attackers to perform user-assisted code execution by crafting an RSS item that includes shell code in its title and/or URL.

undesired_operation.description: Title, description, and feed_title are interpolated into a shell command line without proper shell escaping. stfl::quote is a UI-layer quoting function (for the STFL terminal UI library), not a shell quoting function. An attacker who controls an RSS feed's title, item title, or item description can inject arbitrary shell commands.
code_snippets:
std::string cmdline = strprintf::fmt("%s '%s' %s %s %s",
		                                       bookmark_cmd,
		                                       utils::replace_all(url,"'", "%27"),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(title)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(description)),
		                                       quote_empty(stfl::quote(feed_title)));
undesired_operation.impact: Arbitrary command execution — a malicious RSS feed can set a title or description containing shell metacharacters (e.g. $(malicious_command) or `malicious_command`) which will be executed by /bin/sh when the user bookmarks the item.

step_by_step_execution.description: bookmark_cmd is read from configuration

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: User has configured a bookmark command

cmdline is passed to /bin/sh -c for execution, which interprets shell metacharacters

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly describes CVE-2017-12904: the title, description, and feed_title parameters are not properly shell-escaped before being interpolated into a command line passed to /bin/sh, allowing OS command injection via crafted RSS items. The fix replaces stfl::quote/quote_empty with proper single-quoting and replace_all for single quotes.

actual_issue_summary: The bookmark function passed title, description, and feed_title to a shell command without proper shell escaping. stfl::quote is a UI library function, not a shell escaping function, and the format string lacked single quotes around those arguments. This allowed an attacker to craft RSS items with shell metacharacters in titles/descriptions to achieve command execution when a user bookmarks the item. The fix wraps all arguments in single quotes and escapes embedded single quotes with %27.

cve_desc: Integer underflow in the cli_scanpe function in pe.c in ClamAV before 0.97.8 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a skewed offset larger than the size of the PE section in a UPX packed executable, which triggers an out-of-bounds read.

undesired_operation.description: When len is 1, a 1-byte heap buffer is allocated, but after the loop terminates with i=2, the null-terminator write name[i/2] = name[1] = '\0' writes one byte past the end of the allocated buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow by one byte (off-by-one). Can corrupt heap metadata, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: len is 1, so it's non-zero
1 is not > 400

step_by_step_execution.description: cli_malloc(len) allocates 1 byte on the heap
Loop executes: i=0, 0<1, name[0]=name[0], i becomes 2. Then 2<1 is false, loop ends with i=2.
name[i/2] = name[1] = '\0' writes at index 1, which is past the end of the 1-byte buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the off-by-one heap buffer overflow that the commit fixes. The allocation of cli_malloc(len) is too small because the null terminator write at name[i/2] can write one byte past the buffer when len is odd. The fix changes cli_malloc(len) to cli_malloc(len+1). While the CVE description references pe.c/cli_scanpe, this commit specifically addresses this sis.c bug as part of the ClamAV 0.97.8 security fixes, and the finding accurately describes the vulnerability the diff resolves.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one heap buffer overflow in getsistring() in sis.c: cli_malloc(len) allocates a buffer that is one byte too small for the null terminator written by name[i/2]='\0' after the conversion loop. The fix changes the allocation to cli_malloc(len+1).

cve_desc: A buffer overflow was discovered in the URL-authentication backend of the Icecast before 2.4.4. If the backend is enabled, then any malicious HTTP client can send a request for that specific resource including a crafted header, leading to denial of service and potentially remote code execution.

undesired_operation.description: snprintf with %s format reads from (char*)ptr+22 until a null terminator is found, but libcurl's header callback does not guarantee null-termination of ptr. This causes a heap buffer over-read past the ptr buffer boundary, potentially leaking adjacent heap memory into url->errormsg.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure — adjacent heap contents may be copied into url->errormsg which could be sent back to a client, or denial of service via crash if read hits unmapped memory.

state.value: attacker-controlled header data, not necessarily null-terminated

initial_state.value: pointer to header data from libcurl callback, not null-terminated

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: A valid authenticated client triggers this code path
Attacker crafts a header starting with 'icecast-auth-message: '

step_by_step_execution.description: snprintf copies from ptr+22 using %s format, reading until null terminator without any length bound on the source data from ptr

in_state.value: header data from curl, not null-terminated, length known only via size*nmemb
not checked or used to bound the read

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies a key component of CVE-2018-18820: the snprintf call reads from ptr+22 using %s without respecting the actual buffer length (size*nmemb). The fix replaces this with length-bounded memcpy using the known length. This is one of the buffer overflow issues in the URL auth code that the CVE describes.

actual_issue_summary: Buffer overflows in Icecast's URL authentication backend's cURL header callback. The function handle_returned_header did not validate the length of incoming header data (size*nmemb) before using strncasecmp and snprintf/sscanf operations on it. The fix adds length checks before all string comparisons and replaces unbounded snprintf(%s) with length-bounded memcpy for the error message header processing.

cve_desc: Integer overflow in the sctp_setsockopt_auth_key function in net/sctp/socket.c in the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (sctp) implementation in the Linux kernel 2.6.24-rc1 through 2.6.26.3 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (panic) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a crafted sca_keylength field associated with the SCTP_AUTH_KEY option.

undesired_operation.description: The check `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen` fails to subtract the size of the sctp_authkey header. Since the key data begins at authkey->sca_key (after the header fields), the actual available key bytes in the allocation are `optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)`. By setting sca_keylength up to optlen, the downstream sctp_auth_set_key function will read sca_keylength bytes from authkey->sca_key, overreading the heap-allocated buffer by up to sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer over-read leading to information disclosure of adjacent heap memory or potential use of attacker-influenced key material beyond the allocated buffer, which could also lead to a crash.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same vulnerability as CVE-2008-3526: the bounds check compares sca_keylength against optlen instead of optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey), allowing an overflow/over-read of up to sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes. This matches the diff fix exactly. While the CVE emphasizes the integer overflow aspect (CWE-189), the finding describes the direct consequence of the incorrect bounds check, which is the same underlying problem.

actual_issue_summary: The bounds check `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen` was incorrect because it should have been `authkey->sca_keylength > optlen - sizeof(struct sctp_authkey)`. The key data starts after the sctp_authkey header, so the check allowed sca_keylength to be up to sizeof(struct sctp_authkey) bytes larger than the actual key data in the buffer, leading to a buffer over-read/overflow of up to 8 bytes.

cve_desc: A memory leak in the mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq() function in drivers/net/ethernet/mellanox/mlx5/core/fpga/conn.c in the Linux kernel before 5.3.11 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption) by triggering mlx5_vector2eqn() failures, aka CID-c8c2a057fdc7.

undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service through kernel memory exhaustion if the error path is triggered repeatedly.

actual_issue_summary: Memory leak in mlx5_fpga_conn_create_cq(): when mlx5_vector2eqn() fails, the previously kvzalloc'd 'in' buffer is not freed before jumping to the err_cqwq error handler, causing a memory leak.

cve_desc: An issue was discovered in net/tipc/crypto.c in the Linux kernel before 5.14.16. The Transparent Inter-Process Communication (TIPC) functionality allows remote attackers to exploit insufficient validation of user-supplied sizes for the MSG_CRYPTO message type.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2021-43267: In tipc_crypto_key_rcv(), the keylen field from MSG_CRYPTO messages was not validated before being used in memcpy to copy key data into a heap buffer sized by msg_data_sz. An attacker could supply a large keylen value causing a heap buffer overflow. The fix adds validation that the message size is sufficient and that keylen is consistent with the message size before any memory allocation or copy.

actual_issue_summary: Missing bounds check in lzw_decode allows code_new to equal or exceed (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) when current_entry has grown to that value, causing out-of-bounds access on the dictionary table. The fix adds an explicit check rejecting code_new >= (1 << LZW_CODE_MAX) as LZW_BAD_CODE.

cve_desc: arch/powerpc/kvm/book3s_rtas.c in the Linux kernel through 5.13.5 on the powerpc platform allows KVM guest OS users to cause host OS memory corruption via rtas_args.nargs, aka CID-f62f3c20647e.
diff:
--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -25,6 +25,17 @@
 	 * value so we can restore it on the way out.
 	 */
 	orig_rets = args.rets;
+	if (be32_to_cpu(args.nargs) >= ARRAY_SIZE(args.args)) {
+		/*
+		 * Don't overflow our args array: ensure there is room for
+		 * at least rets[0] (even if the call specifies 0 nret).
+		 *
+		 * Each handler must then check for the correct nargs and nret
+		 * values, but they may always return failure in rets[0].
+		 */
+		rc = -EINVAL;
+		goto fail;
+	}
 	args.rets = &args.args[be32_to_cpu(args.nargs)];
 
 	mutex_lock(&vcpu->kvm->arch.rtas_token_lock);
@@ -52,9 +63,17 @@
 fail:
 	/*
 	 * We only get here if the guest has called RTAS with a bogus
-	 * args pointer. That means we can't get to the args, and so we
-	 * can't fail the RTAS call. So fail right out to userspace,
-	 * which should kill the guest.
+	 * args pointer or nargs/nret values that would overflow the
+	 * array. That means we can't get to the args, and so we can't
+	 * fail the RTAS call. So fail right out to userspace, which
+	 * should kill the guest.
+	 *
+	 * SLOF should actually pass the hcall return value from the
+	 * rtas handler call in r3, so enter_rtas could be modified to
+	 * return a failure indication in r3 and we could return such
+	 * errors to the guest rather than failing to host userspace.
+	 * However old guests that don't test for failure could then
+	 * continue silently after errors, so for now we won't do this.
 	 */
 	return rc;
 }

undesired_operation.description: args.rets is set to &args.args[be32_to_cpu(args.nargs)] where args.nargs is guest-controlled. The args.args array has a fixed size (typically 16 elements). If nargs exceeds that size, args.rets points outside the stack-allocated args structure. When the RTAS handler writes return values through args.rets, it corrupts adjacent stack memory.
undesired_operation.impact: A malicious guest can corrupt kernel stack memory by providing a large nargs value, potentially achieving host kernel code execution from the guest (guest-to-host escape).

step_by_step_execution.description: Guest physical address of RTAS args is read from register r4 and args struct is copied from guest memory into the stack-allocated host args struct.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The guest provides a valid args_phys address so kvm_read_guest succeeds.

step_by_step_execution.description: orig_rets saves the guest rets pointer, then args.rets is set to &args.args[be32_to_cpu(args.nargs)] with no bounds check on nargs.

in_state.value: guest-controlled large value, e.g. be32 encoding of 100

out_state.value: points far outside args.args[] array (out-of-bounds on the stack)

step_by_step_execution.description: Handler is invoked for a matching token; handler writes failure value -3 to rets[0], which writes to memory outside the args structure on the stack.

out_state.value: corrupted by write of 0xfffffffd to arbitrary offset

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely describes the CVE-2021-37576 vulnerability: guest-controlled args.nargs is not bounds-checked before being used to compute args.rets, allowing an out-of-bounds write that corrupts host memory. The fix adds exactly this bounds check.

actual_issue_summary: Guest-controlled rtas_args.nargs is used without bounds checking to compute the args.rets pointer as &args.args[be32_to_cpu(args.nargs)]. When nargs exceeds the args.args array size, rets points outside the array. RTAS handlers that detect incorrect nargs/nret values write a -3 failure indicator to rets[0], which corrupts host kernel stack memory. The fix adds a check that nargs < ARRAY_SIZE(args.args) before computing the rets pointer.

cve_desc: Integer signedness error in the simplestring_addn function in simplestring.c in xmlrpc-epi through 0.54.2, as used in PHP before 5.5.38, 5.6.x before 5.6.24, and 7.x before 7.0.9, allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (heap-based buffer overflow) or possibly have unspecified other impact via a long first argument to the PHP xmlrpc_encode_request function.

undesired_operation.description: memcpy is called with add_len cast to size_t. When add_len is negative, it becomes a very large unsigned value (e.g., 0xFFFFFFFF for -1), causing a massive heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or crash. An attacker supplying a negative add_len causes memcpy to copy gigabytes of data.

state.value: negative int value, e.g. -1, which becomes a huge size_t when passed to memcpy

initial_state.value: negative int from caller (e.g. due to long xmlrpc_encode_request argument)

step_by_step_execution.description: target and source are checked for non-NULL

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With a negative add_len, the sum target->len + add_len + 1 can be smaller than target->size, so the realloc block is skipped entirely — no buffer resize occurs.
target->str was already initialized, so this check passes
add_len is negative (non-zero), so this check passes

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy is called with add_len implicitly converted to size_t, turning the negative int into a huge unsigned value, causing a heap buffer overflow

in_state.value: pointer to heap buffer of target->size bytes

out_state.value: corrupted — memcpy writes far beyond allocated buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2016-6296: the add_len parameter is a signed int, and when a negative value is passed, it bypasses the size check but gets implicitly converted to a huge size_t in the memcpy call, causing a heap buffer overflow. The fix changes add_len to size_t and adds overflow checks.

actual_issue_summary: The add_len parameter of simplestring_addn was declared as int (signed). A very long input string could result in a negative add_len due to integer overflow, which would bypass the buffer size check but then be implicitly converted to a very large size_t when passed to memcpy, causing a heap-based buffer overflow. The fix changes add_len to size_t and adds explicit overflow checks.

cve_desc: gio/gsocketclient.c in GNOME GLib 2.59.2 does not ensure that a parent GTask remains alive during the execution of a connection-attempting enumeration, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (g_socket_client_connected_callback mishandling and application crash) via a crafted web site, as demonstrated by GNOME Web (aka Epiphany).

undesired_operation.description: When g_socket_connection_connect_finish fails and the attempt's cancellable is not cancelled, the code dereferences data->connectable without checking if data is NULL. data can be NULL because another successful connection attempt sets attempt->data = NULL for all remaining attempts.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via NULL pointer dereference crash. An attacker who can influence network timing (e.g., causing one connection to succeed while another fails shortly after) can trigger this crash.

initial_state.value: attempt->data, which could be NULL if another connection succeeded and set attempt_entry->data = NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: data is assigned from attempt->data, which may be NULL if another connection attempt succeeded first and set this attempt's data pointer to NULL

in_state.value: NULL (set by successful connection's cleanup loop)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: data is NULL, so short-circuit evaluation makes the whole condition false
The connection attempt failed (e.g., timeout or refused)
The attempt's cancellable may not yet be cancelled depending on timing

step_by_step_execution.description: Code dereferences data->connectable via clarify_connect_error, but data is NULL, causing a crash

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core issue of CVE-2019-9633: the parent GTask/data can become invalid (NULL) while connection attempts are still in progress. The vulnerable code sets attempt_entry->data = NULL for other attempts when one succeeds, and subsequent callbacks on those attempts dereference data without adequate NULL checks, leading to a crash. The fix removes the data=NULL assignment and instead uses proper cancellation/completion checks to keep the task alive.

actual_issue_summary: The parent GTask is not kept alive during connection-attempting enumeration. When one connection attempt succeeds, the cleanup loop sets attempt_entry->data = NULL for remaining attempts. When those attempts' callbacks later fire, they can dereference the NULL data pointer, causing a crash. The fix replaces the data=NULL pattern with proper cancellation checking (via task_completed_or_cancelled) and ensures proper reference counting of the GTask.

cve_desc: Buffer overflow in the hfsplus_find_cat function in fs/hfsplus/catalog.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 allows attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption or system crash) via an hfsplus filesystem image with an invalid catalog namelength field, related to the hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni function.

undesired_operation.description: tmp.thread.nodeName is read from disk (untrusted filesystem data) and passed directly to hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni without validating that nodeName.length is within the valid range (0..255). hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni will use the unchecked length to copy unicode characters into the fixed-size search key buffer, causing a buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: A crafted HFS+ filesystem image can trigger a heap or stack buffer overflow when the catalog is traversed, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution in kernel context or denial of service via kernel crash.

initial_state.value: pointer to allocated search key buffer with fixed-size name field (max 255 unicode chars)
catalog node ID passed by caller
superblock of the mounted hfsplus filesystem

step_by_step_execution.description: hfsplus_cat_build_key is called to build an initial search key from cnid

out_state.value: populated with initial key for cnid lookup

step_by_step_execution.description: hfs_brec_read reads the catalog entry from disk into tmp

out_state.value: populated from on-disk data, including tmp.thread.nodeName.length which is attacker-controlled

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: For the vulnerability path to be reached, the read must succeed (err == 0)

step_by_step_execution.description: type is read from tmp.type

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: For the vulnerability to be reached, the type must be a valid thread type so execution continues past this check

step_by_step_execution.description: hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni is called with the unchecked nodeName from disk. It uses nodeName.length to memcpy unicode characters into the fixed-size (255 chars) name field in fd->search_key, overflowing the buffer.

in_state.value: points to buffer with name field limited to 255 unicode chars

out_state.value: buffer overflowed, memory corruption occurs beyond the name field

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly describes CVE-2008-4933: the missing validation of tmp.thread.nodeName.length before passing it to hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni, leading to a buffer overflow. The fix adds exactly this check (length > 255).

actual_issue_summary: In hfsplus_find_cat(), the catalog thread's nodeName.length field read from a corrupted HFS+ filesystem image is not validated before being passed to hfsplus_cat_build_key_uni(). If length exceeds 255, the memcpy inside that function overflows the fixed-size name buffer in the search key, causing memory corruption or a kernel crash. The fix adds a check that returns -EIO if nodeName.length > 255.

cve_desc: pax_decode_header in sparse.c in GNU Tar before 1.32 had a NULL pointer dereference when parsing certain archives that have malformed extended headers.

undesired_operation.description: When find_next_block() returns NULL inside the COPY_BUF macro (e.g., due to a truncated archive), the code dereferences the NULL pointer via b->buffer, causing a crash.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (NULL pointer dereference) when processing a maliciously truncated sparse tar archive.

state.value: NULL (find_next_block() returned NULL due to unexpected EOF)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: A malformed sparse archive has sparse_major > 0, so the main body executes.

step_by_step_execution.description: set_next_block_after(current_header) is called, then blk = find_next_block() is called. In the vulnerable version there is no NULL check on blk here either, but assume it succeeds for this path.

COPY_BUF macro begins iterating. When src reaches endp (end of current block), set_next_block_after(b) is called and then b = find_next_block(). If the archive is truncated, find_next_block() returns NULL.

out_state.value: NULL (find_next_block returned NULL due to truncated archive)

step_by_step_execution.description: The code immediately dereferences b via src = b->buffer, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crash.

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2019-9923: a NULL pointer dereference in pax_decode_header when find_next_block() returns NULL due to malformed/truncated archives. The fix adds NULL checks after both calls to find_next_block(). The CWE-476 also matches.

actual_issue_summary: In pax_decode_header in sparse.c, find_next_block() can return NULL when the archive is truncated or has malformed extended headers. The return value was not checked before dereferencing, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds NULL checks after both call sites of find_next_block() (one in the main function body and one inside the COPY_BUF macro).

cve_desc: The bmexec_trans function in kwset.c in grep 2.19 through 2.21 allows local users to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds heap read and crash) via crafted input when using the -F option.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in '12 * len' and '11 * len' (both computed as int) causes the fast-loop guard 'size > 12 * len' to be true when it shouldn't be, and causes 'ep = text + size - 11 * len' to point far past the text buffer, leading to out-of-bounds reads in the skip loop.
code_snippets: for (ep = text + size - 11 * len; tp <= ep; )
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds heap read past the text buffer. An attacker providing a large enough pattern and text can read adjacent memory, causing information disclosure or a crash (denial of service).

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2015-1345) is caused by memchr_kwset advancing tp past ep, after which bm_delta2_search is called with tp > ep, leading to out-of-bounds heap reads. The fix adds 'if (ep <= tp) break;' after memchr_kwset returns. The finding instead describes a hypothetical integer overflow in '12 * len' / '11 * len' computations, which is a completely different issue and not the root cause of this CVE. The pattern length (len = kwset->mind) would need to be enormous to overflow an int multiplication, and the actual vulnerability is triggered with small patterns (e.g., 19-byte pattern as shown in the commit message).

actual_issue_summary: In bmexec_trans, after memchr_kwset advances tp, tp can end up past ep. The code then falls through to call bm_delta2_search with tp > ep, causing out-of-bounds heap reads. The fix adds a check 'if (ep <= tp) break;' after memchr_kwset to prevent this.

cve_desc: The kssl_keytab_is_available function in ssl/kssl.c in OpenSSL before 0.9.8n, when Kerberos is enabled but Kerberos configuration files cannot be opened, does not check a certain return value, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash) via SSL cipher negotiation, as demonstrated by a chroot installation of Dovecot or stunnel without Kerberos configuration files inside the chroot.

--- vulnerable
+++ fixed
@@ -31,6 +31,9 @@

                                      KRB5_NT_SRV_HST, &princ);
 
+    if (krb5rc)
+	goto exit;
+
     krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                 princ,
                                 0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,

krb5rc = krb5_kt_get_entry(krb5context, krb5keytab, 
                                princ,
                                0 /* IGNORE_VNO */,
                                0 /* IGNORE_ENCTYPE */,
                                &entry);
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (NULL pointer dereference). An attacker who can influence DNS resolution or Kerberos configuration to make krb5_sname_to_principal fail can trigger this.

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_init_context succeeds, setting krb5context to a valid context

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: krb5_init_context succeeded so krb5rc is 0

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_kt_default or krb5_kt_resolve succeeds, setting krb5keytab
krb5_sname_to_principal fails because Kerberos config files are not available (e.g. chroot without /etc/krb5.conf)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: There is no check of krb5rc after krb5_sname_to_principal in the vulnerable code - execution falls through directly to krb5_kt_get_entry

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_kt_get_entry is called with princ=NULL, causing NULL pointer dereference

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2010-0433: the missing check of the return value from krb5_sname_to_principal leads to a NULL princ being passed to krb5_kt_get_entry, causing a NULL pointer dereference and daemon crash. The fix adds exactly that check.

actual_issue_summary: The return value of krb5_sname_to_principal was not checked in kssl_keytab_is_available. If it failed (e.g. due to missing Kerberos configuration files in a chroot), princ remained NULL and was passed to krb5_kt_get_entry, causing a NULL pointer dereference and crash. The fix adds a check of krb5rc after krb5_sname_to_principal, jumping to the exit label on failure.

cve_desc: Multiple double free vulnerabilities in the (1) agent_sign_data function in agent.c, (2) channel_request function in channels.c, (3) ssh_userauth_pubkey function in auth.c, (4) sftp_parse_attr_3 function in sftp.c, and (5) try_publickey_from_file function in keyfiles.c in libssh before 0.5.3 allow remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) and possibly execute arbitrary code via unspecified vectors.

undesired_operation.description: When any of the buffer_add or sftp_packet_write calls fail, ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) are called inside the if-block, then called again unconditionally on the lines immediately after, resulting in a double-free.
undesired_operation.impact: Double-free can corrupt heap metadata, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service via crash.

step_by_step_execution.description: buffer_add_u32, buffer_add_ssh_string, buffer_add_attributes, or sftp_packet_write returns < 0

step_by_step_execution.condition: buffer_add_u32(buffer, id) < 0 || buffer_add_ssh_string(buffer, path) < 0 || buffer_add_attributes(buffer, &attr) < 0 || sftp_packet_write(sftp, SSH_FXP_MKDIR, buffer) < 0
step_by_step_execution.reasoning: One of the buffer operations or packet write failed, so the error-handling block is entered

step_by_step_execution.description: Inside the if-block, ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) are called, freeing both objects
Execution falls through (no return) to the unconditional ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) calls, freeing already-freed memory

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the double-free vulnerability in sftp_mkdir. The missing 'return -1' in the error-handling block causes buffer and path to be freed twice. This matches CVE-2012-4559 which describes multiple double free vulnerabilities in libssh, including in sftp.c.

actual_issue_summary: In sftp_mkdir, when buffer_add or sftp_packet_write fails, the error-handling block frees buffer and path but does not return, causing execution to fall through to unconditional ssh_buffer_free(buffer) and ssh_string_free(path) calls, resulting in a double-free. The fix adds 'return -1' to the error block.

cve_desc: ASN.1 strings are represented internally within OpenSSL as an ASN1_STRING structure which contains a buffer holding the string data and a field holding the buffer length. This contrasts with normal C strings which are repesented as a buffer for the string data which is terminated with a NUL (0) byte. Although not a strict requirement, ASN.1 strings that are parsed using OpenSSL's own "d2i" functions (and other similar parsing functions) as well as any string whose value has been set with the ASN1_STRING_set() function will additionally NUL terminate the byte array in the ASN1_STRING structure. However, it is possible for applications to directly construct valid ASN1_STRING structures which do not NUL terminate the byte array by directly setting the "data" and "length" fields in the ASN1_STRING array. This can also happen by using the ASN1_STRING_set0() function. Numerous OpenSSL functions that print ASN.1 data have been found to assume that the ASN1_STRING byte array will be NUL terminated, even though this is not guaranteed for strings that have been directly constructed. Where an application requests an ASN.1 structure to be printed, and where that ASN.1 structure contains ASN1_STRINGs that have been directly constructed by the application without NUL terminating the "data" field, then a read buffer overrun can occur. The same thing can also occur during name constraints processing of certificates (for example if a certificate has been directly constructed by the application instead of loading it via the OpenSSL parsing functions, and the certificate contains non NUL terminated ASN1_STRING structures). It can also occur in the X509_get1_email(), X509_REQ_get1_email() and X509_get1_ocsp() functions. If a malicious actor can cause an application to directly construct an ASN1_STRING and then process it through one of the affected OpenSSL functions then this issue could be hit. This might result in a crash (causing a Denial of Service attack). It could also result in the disclosure of private memory contents (such as private keys, or sensitive plaintext). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.1.1l (Affected 1.1.1-1.1.1k). Fixed in OpenSSL 1.0.2za (Affected 1.0.2-1.0.2y).

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds read of params->base->data[0] when params->base->length is 0 but params->base->data is a non-NULL pointer (e.g., a zero-length allocation). The code checks that data is non-NULL but never checks that length >= 1 before dereferencing data[0].
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure (one byte heap OOB read) or crash/denial of service via a crafted EC parameters structure with a zero-length base point octet string.

step_by_step_execution.description: The validation check tests !params->order || !params->base || !params->base->data

out_state.value: true (all three are non-NULL, but length is not checked)

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: All three pointers are non-NULL, so the check passes and execution continues past the error branch. The missing check for params->base->length == 0 allows a zero-length buffer to proceed.

step_by_step_execution.description: Code accesses params->base->data[0] to set point conversion form, reading one byte beyond the zero-length buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the fix in the diff: the vulnerable code failed to check params->base->length == 0, allowing an out-of-bounds read of params->base->data[0] when the base octet string has zero length. This is the specific instance of CWE-125 (read buffer overrun) that CVE-2021-3712 addresses in this particular commit.

actual_issue_summary: The function EC_GROUP_new_from_ecparameters did not check that params->base->length is non-zero before accessing params->base->data[0]. When params->base is a valid ASN1_STRING with length 0 but a non-NULL data pointer, this results in a read buffer overrun (CWE-125). The fix adds a check for params->base->length == 0 alongside the existing NULL checks.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow in the GmfOpenMesh() function of libMeshb v7.61 allows attackers to cause a Denial of Service (DoS) via a crafted MESH file.

undesired_operation.description: fscanf with %s format specifier reads an unbounded string from the file into the fixed-size stack buffer 'str' (size GmfStrSiz), causing a stack buffer overflow when a token in the crafted MESH file exceeds GmfStrSiz-1 bytes.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack-based buffer overflow leading to denial of service (crash) or potential arbitrary code execution via a crafted MESH file.

initial_state.value: uninitialized stack buffer of size GmfStrSiz

step_by_step_execution.description: File is opened for reading in ASCII mode (.mesh extension), msh->hdl is set via fopen

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The file has .mesh extension so typ is Asc | MshFil, thus the Bin bit is not set, and execution enters the else (ASCII) branch.

step_by_step_execution.description: fscanf(msh->hdl, "%s", str) reads a whitespace-delimited token from the file into str with no length bound. A crafted file contains a token longer than GmfStrSiz-1 bytes, overflowing the stack buffer.

out_state.value: overflowed beyond GmfStrSiz bytes, corrupting adjacent stack memory

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the CVE-2021-46225 vulnerability: unbounded fscanf("%s") into a fixed-size stack buffer str, which was fixed by changing to "%100s" to limit the read length. The CWE-120 matches the NVD classification.

actual_issue_summary: In GmfOpenMesh(), two fscanf calls used "%s" format to read tokens into a fixed-size stack buffer 'str' of size GmfStrSiz without length limits. A crafted .mesh file with an oversized token causes a stack buffer overflow. The fix changes "%s" to "%100s" to limit the number of characters read.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The Grappler optimizer in TensorFlow can be used to cause a denial of service by altering a `SavedModel` such that `IsSimplifiableReshape` would trigger `CHECK` failures. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When outputs[0]->dtype() is not DT_INT32, the code unconditionally interprets the tensor data as int64_t via outputs[0]->flat<int64_t>(). If the actual dtype is something else (e.g., DT_FLOAT, DT_STRING), this is a type confusion leading to reading memory with the wrong interpretation or crashing.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure. A constant node with a dtype other than DT_INT32 or DT_INT64 (e.g., DT_FLOAT) will have its raw data reinterpreted as int64_t, which may cause assertion failures in flat<>() or produce garbage values passed to MakeShape.

initial_state.value: any dtype from evaluated constant node

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If dtype is something other than DT_INT32, e.g. DT_FLOAT, the else branch is taken

step_by_step_execution.description: Data is reinterpreted as int64_t regardless of actual dtype

out_state.value: vector of garbage int64_t values from reinterpreted float data

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-23581) is about TF_CHECK_OK being used instead of proper error handling for MakeShape. When MakeShape returns a non-OK status (e.g., due to an invalid shape from a crafted SavedModel), TF_CHECK_OK triggers a CHECK failure causing an abort/crash (CWE-617). The fix replaces TF_CHECK_OK with graceful error propagation. The finding describes a hypothetical type confusion issue that is unrelated to the actual vulnerability fixed.

actual_issue_summary: TF_CHECK_OK was used on the return value of TensorShapeUtils::MakeShape, which causes a CHECK failure (process abort) if MakeShape returns an error (e.g., due to invalid shape dimensions from a crafted SavedModel). The fix replaces TF_CHECK_OK with proper error status propagation, returning the error gracefully instead of crashing.

cve_desc: PJSIP is a free and open source multimedia communication library. In version 2.11.1 and prior, if incoming RTCP XR message contain block, the data field is not checked against the received packet size, potentially resulting in an out-of-bound read access. This affects all users that use PJMEDIA and RTCP XR. A malicious actor can send a RTCP XR message with an invalid packet size.

The code reads fields (e.g., ntp_sec, ntp_frac, or numerous voip_mtc fields) from these pointers without verifying the block's rb_len is large enough for the specific struct, causing out-of-bounds reads.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of heap/stack contents adjacent to the packet buffer; potential crash/denial of service if the read crosses a page boundary into unmapped memory.

in_state.value: Points to a location where the header is within packet bounds but full struct extends beyond pkt+size

out_state.value: Contains data read from out-of-bounds memory

judgments.reasoning: This finding precisely describes the CVE-2021-43845 vulnerability: RTCP XR report block pointers are set without validating that the full struct fits within the received packet size, leading to out-of-bound reads (CWE-125). The fix adds exactly these bounds checks before assigning each pointer.

actual_issue_summary: In pjmedia_rtcp_xr_rx_rtcp_xr(), when parsing RTCP XR report blocks, the code assigns pointers (rb_rr_time, rb_dlrr, rb_stats, rb_voip_mtc) to report block data without checking that the full struct size fits within the received packet buffer. This allows an attacker to craft a packet with a valid header but insufficient data, causing out-of-bounds reads when the struct fields are later accessed. The fix adds bounds checks (rb_hdr + sizeof(struct) <= pkt + size) before each pointer assignment.

cve_desc: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c in the Linux kernel before 2.6.28-rc1 does not check a certain return value from the read_mapping_page function before calling kmap, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service (system crash) via a crafted hfsplus filesystem image.

undesired_operation.description: read_mapping_page() can return an ERR_PTR on I/O error or allocation failure, but the return value is never checked. The ERR_PTR (a small negative value cast to pointer, e.g., 0xFFFFFFF0) is then passed directly to kmap(), which dereferences it as a struct page pointer, causing an invalid memory access in the kernel.
code_snippets:
page = read_mapping_page(mapping, offset / PAGE_CACHE_BITS, NULL);
		curr = pptr = kmap(page);

page = read_mapping_page(mapping, offset / PAGE_CACHE_BITS, NULL);
	pptr = kmap(page);
undesired_operation.impact: Kernel crash (denial of service) or potential code execution. An attacker can trigger this by mounting a crafted HFS+ filesystem image that causes I/O errors during bitmap page reads, or by causing memory pressure during allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: read_mapping_page() is called and returns an ERR_PTR due to I/O error on crafted HFS+ image

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The vulnerable code has no IS_ERR check, so execution continues unconditionally to kmap()

step_by_step_execution.description: kmap(page) is called with the ERR_PTR value, which dereferences it as a struct page pointer, causing a kernel page fault

judgments.reasoning: This finding directly matches CVE-2008-4934: the missing check of read_mapping_page() return value before calling kmap() in hfsplus_block_allocate, leading to denial of service via crafted HFS+ image. The fix adds exactly these IS_ERR checks.

undesired_operation.description: In the block allocation loop (after 'found:'), when advancing to a new page, end is unconditionally set to pptr + PAGE_CACHE_BITS / 32, ignoring whether the bitmap size boundary falls within this page. This allows reading and writing bitmap data beyond the logical end of the bitmap, corrupting adjacent filesystem metadata stored in the same page.
undesired_operation.impact: Filesystem metadata corruption, potentially leading to further exploitation when the corrupted metadata is later interpreted by the kernel. On a crafted HFS+ image, this allows writing 0xFFFFFFFF words beyond the intended bitmap boundary.

state.value: pptr + PAGE_CACHE_BITS / 32 (full page), potentially beyond bitmap size boundary

step_by_step_execution.description: In the allocation loop after found:, when curr reaches end and a new page is read, end is set to full page size

judgments.reasoning: This finding describes a potential out-of-bounds write issue in the bitmap allocation loop, which is unrelated to CVE-2008-4934. The CVE is specifically about missing return value checks on read_mapping_page() before calling kmap(). The diff only adds IS_ERR checks and does not modify the end calculation logic.

actual_issue_summary: The hfsplus_block_allocate function in fs/hfsplus/bitmap.c does not check if read_mapping_page() returns an error (ERR_PTR) before passing the result to kmap(). On a crafted HFS+ filesystem image, read_mapping_page() can fail and return an error pointer, which when passed to kmap() causes an invalid memory dereference and kernel crash (denial of service). The fix adds IS_ERR() checks after all three read_mapping_page() calls in the function.

cve_desc: The binary MP4Box in Gpac 1.0.1 has a double-free vulnerability in the gf_text_get_utf8_line function in load_text.c, which allows attackers to cause a denial of service, even code execution and escalation of privileges.

undesired_operation.description: In the byte-swap loop for unicode_type==2/3, the loop increments i by 2 and checks szLine[i] and szLine[i+1] for double-null termination. If the file data read by gf_fgets does not contain a double-null within lineSize bytes, i can exceed lineSize, reading and writing out of bounds of szLine.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read and write on the heap (szLine buffer), potentially leading to code execution or crash

state.value: exceeds lineSize due to missing double-null terminator

initial_state.value: buffer of lineSize bytes, zeroed then filled by gf_fgets

step_by_step_execution.description: szLine is zeroed and filled by gf_fgets

out_state.value: filled with file data up to lineSize bytes

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Attacker provides a file with the appropriate BOM to trigger this code path

step_by_step_execution.description: While loop iterates checking for double-null, incrementing i by 2 each iteration

out_state.value: potentially exceeds lineSize if no double-null found

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE fix increases szLineConv buffer from 1024 to 2048 bytes, addressing a stack buffer overflow where UTF-8 conversion output (via gf_utf8_wcstombs or the non-UTF8 win-CP expansion loop) could exceed the 1024-byte szLineConv buffer. The finding describes a different potential issue in the byte-swap loop, which is not what the commit fixes. The memset zeroing of szLine before gf_fgets also makes the double-null termination guaranteed within the buffer, so the described issue is also not practically exploitable.

actual_issue_summary: The szLineConv stack buffer was 1024 bytes, but could receive more data than that during UTF-8 conversion (either from the non-UTF8 character expansion loop where j can grow larger than len, or from gf_utf8_wcstombs). The fix increases szLineConv to 2048 bytes and updates the gf_utf8_wcstombs size parameter accordingly, preventing a stack buffer overflow.

cve_desc: MP4Box is a component of GPAC-2.0.0, which is a widely-used third-party package on RPM Fusion. When MP4Box tries to parse a MP4 file, it calls the function `diST_box_read()` to read from video. In this function, it allocates a buffer `str` with fixed length. However, content read from `bs` is controllable by user, so is the length, which causes a buffer overflow.

undesired_operation.description: The while loop reads bytes from the bitstream into the stack buffer str[1024] without bounds checking on the index i, allowing writes past the end of the buffer if more than 1023 non-null bytes are read before a null terminator.
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or crash. An attacker can craft a malicious media file with a diST box containing more than 1024 bytes before a null terminator.

step_by_step_execution.description: i is initialized to 0 and str[0] is set to 0
Loop reads bytes from bs one at a time into str[i], incrementing i each iteration with no bounds check against 1024

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: There is no such bounds check in the code. The loop only breaks on a null byte from the bitstream. If the attacker-controlled input contains >= 1024 non-null bytes, i will exceed the buffer size.

step_by_step_execution.description: str[i] = gf_bs_read_u8(bs) writes beyond the 1024-byte stack buffer

out_state.value: attacker-controlled byte written out of bounds on the stack

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches CVE-2022-1441: the fixed-size stack buffer str[1024] in diST_box_read() can be overflowed because the while loop has no bounds check on i, and the input data length is attacker-controlled. The fix removes the fixed buffer entirely and allocates based on s->size.

actual_issue_summary: In diST_box_read(), a fixed 1024-byte stack buffer is used to read user-controlled data from the bitstream in a loop with no bounds checking, causing a stack buffer overflow when more than 1024 bytes are provided before a null terminator. The fix replaces the stack buffer with a dynamically allocated buffer sized according to s->size.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of shape inference for `ConcatV2` can be used to trigger a denial of service attack via a segfault caused by a type confusion. The `axis` argument is translated into `concat_dim` in the `ConcatShapeHelper` helper function. Then, a value for `min_rank` is computed based on `concat_dim`. This is then used to validate that the `values` tensor has at least the required rank. However, `WithRankAtLeast` receives the lower bound as a 64-bits value and then compares it against the maximum 32-bits integer value that could be represented. Due to the fact that `min_rank` is a 32-bits value and the value of `axis`, the `rank` argument is a negative value, so the error check is bypassed. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: When concat_dim is a large positive int64 value (e.g., > INT32_MAX), the computation 'concat_dim + 1' is narrowed from int64_t to int for min_rank, causing integer truncation/overflow. This makes min_rank small or negative, so the WithRankAtLeast check passes trivially, and then concat_dim is used as an out-of-bounds index in c->Dim(input, concat_dim) and c->Subshape() calls.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential out-of-bounds read. An attacker who controls the concat_dim tensor value can supply a large integer that bypasses the rank validation, leading to out-of-bounds memory access when used as a dimension index.

state.value: truncated to negative or small int32 value

initial_state.value: unset (will be read from concat_dim_t tensor)
non-null Tensor pointer with attacker-controlled value

step_by_step_execution.description: concat_dim is read from the input tensor as an int64_t value controlled by attacker

in_state.value: non-null Tensor with large int64 value, e.g. 0x7FFFFFFF + 1 = 2147483648

step_by_step_execution.description: min_rank is computed as concat_dim + 1 and truncated to int (32-bit)

out_state.value: negative value due to int32 truncation of 2147483649

step_by_step_execution.condition: min_rank passed to WithRankAtLeast as 64-bit, but originated from truncated 32-bit value
step_by_step_execution.reasoning: WithRankAtLeast receives a negative min_rank (after sign extension to 64-bit), which is always <= any rank, so the rank check is trivially satisfied and bypassed

step_by_step_execution.description: concat_dim is used as index in c->Dim(input, concat_dim) and c->Subshape() causing out-of-bounds access since the rank check was bypassed

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: min_rank is declared as 'int' (32-bit) but is computed from the 64-bit concat_dim, causing truncation that bypasses the WithRankAtLeast validation. This matches exactly the CVE description of a type confusion where a 32-bit value overflows/wraps, bypassing the error check. The fix changes 'const int min_rank' to 'const int64 min_rank'.

actual_issue_summary: The variable min_rank was declared as 'int' (32-bit) but computed from the 64-bit concat_dim value. When concat_dim is large enough, the result truncates to a negative 32-bit value, bypassing the WithRankAtLeast rank validation check and leading to a segfault when the large concat_dim is used as a dimension index. The fix changes min_rank from 'int' to 'int64'.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalAvgPoolGrad` does not consider cases where the input tensors are invalid allowing an attacker to read from outside of bounds of heap. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: No validation that out_batch==in_batch or out_depth==in_depth. When out_batch > in_batch, the loop iterates b up to out_batch-1, computing in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c which exceeds the in_backprop_tensor_temp_mat column count (in_cols * in_rows * in_batch), causing out-of-bounds write.
code_snippets: const int64_t in_index = (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c;
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow write. Attacker sets out_backprop with larger batch dimension than orig_input_tensor_shape specifies, writing past the allocated in_backprop buffer.

step_by_step_execution.description: out_batch and in_batch are read from separate attacker-controlled inputs with no check that they match

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Loop runs with b up to out_batch-1, but in_index computation uses in_rows/in_cols from the smaller input shape

relevant_state.value: (b * in_rows + in_r) * in_cols + in_c, can exceed buffer bounds

judgments.reasoning: The CVE (CVE-2022-21730) is specifically about missing validation that row_pooling_sequence and col_pooling_sequence tensor values are non-negative, leading to out-of-bounds heap reads when negative indices are used. The fix adds checks `in_row_start >= 0 && in_row_end >= 0` and `in_col_start >= 0 && in_col_end >= 0`. This finding describes a different potential issue (batch dimension mismatch) which is not what the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The FractionalAvgPoolGrad implementation did not validate that row_pooling_sequence and col_pooling_sequence tensor values are non-negative. Negative values in these tensors could produce negative indices (in_row_start, in_col_start, in_row_end, in_col_end), leading to out-of-bounds heap memory reads via coeffRef with negative index values. The fix adds OP_REQUIRES checks ensuring these values are >= 0.

cve_desc: Tensorflow is an Open Source Machine Learning Framework. The implementation of `FractionalMaxPool` can be made to crash a TensorFlow process via a division by 0. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.8.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.7.1, TensorFlow 2.6.3, and TensorFlow 2.5.3, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: output_size[i] can be 0 when pooling_ratio_[i] > input_size[i], because DCHECK_GT is a no-op in release builds. This causes the output tensor to have 0 elements, but GeneratePoolingSequence may still return a non-empty sequence, leading to out-of-bounds writes via out_mat.col(out_offset) on a zero-element tensor.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential heap out-of-bounds write, since out_mat is mapped over a zero-element output tensor but indexed with a non-zero offset derived from pooling sequences.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same root cause as CVE-2022-21735: when pooling_ratio is larger than the input dimension size, output_size becomes 0, which leads to a division by zero (inside GeneratePoolingSequence) or other crashes. The fix adds exactly the validation that input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i] to prevent this. While the finding emphasizes OOB write as a consequence and the CVE description emphasizes division by zero, both stem from the same missing input validation that the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: When pooling_ratio is higher than the input dimension size for any dimension, output_size becomes 0 via floor(input_size/pooling_ratio), which causes a division by zero crash (likely inside GeneratePoolingSequence). The fix adds a validation check ensuring input_size[i] >= pooling_ratio_[i] for all dimensions.

cve_desc: There is an assertion failure in SingleComponentLSScan::ParseMCU in singlecomponentlsscan.cpp in libjpeg before 1.64 via an empty JPEG-LS scan.

undesired_operation.description: When m_ulRemaining[0] is 0 and m_pFrame->HeightOf() > 0, 'lines' is 0. The do-while loop 'do { ... } while(--lines)' executes the body once, then decrements lines to -1. Since -1 is non-zero, the loop continues indefinitely, traversing the linked list via line->m_pNext past its end, causing NULL pointer dereferences or out-of-bounds memory reads/writes.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (NULL pointer dereference when line->m_pNext is NULL) or potential out-of-bounds memory access when traversing the linked list beyond its valid elements.

initial_state.value: m_ulRemaining[0], which can be 0 for an empty JPEG-LS scan

step_by_step_execution.description: lines is initialized from m_ulRemaining[0]

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: HeightOf() is > 0, so the DNL fix-up block is skipped
lines is 0, which is not > 8
HeightOf is positive

step_by_step_execution.description: m_ulRemaining[0] -= lines subtracts 0, no change
assert(lines > 0) fails because lines == 0, causing abort in debug builds. In release builds, execution continues to the do-while loop.

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same root cause as CVE-2022-32978: when lines is 0 due to an empty JPEG-LS scan, the vulnerable code hits `assert(lines > 0)` which causes an assertion failure (the CVE description). The finding also correctly describes what would happen in release builds without assertions (infinite loop / crash). The fix replaces the assert with an early return when lines == 0, which is exactly what the finding addresses.

actual_issue_summary: When parsing an empty JPEG-LS scan where m_ulRemaining[0] is 0 and HeightOf() > 0, the variable 'lines' remains 0, triggering `assert(lines > 0)` which causes an assertion failure / program abort. The fix replaces the assert with an early `return false` when lines == 0.

cve_desc: Nginx NJS v0.7.2 was discovered to contain a segmentation violation in the function njs_value_own_enumerate at src/njs_value.c.

undesired_operation.description: Out-of-bounds write via memcpy into array->start[start] in the fast array path. When items > 0 and the array has been shrunk by valueOf side effects, memcpy writes new items at a position computed from the stale 'length', overwriting memory beyond the array's actual allocated buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow write; attacker can overwrite adjacent heap objects, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE (CVE-2022-29779) is about uninitialized entries in the 'deleted' array when Array.prototype.splice() is called on a non-fast-array 'this' that has gaps (missing properties). The fix adds an else branch that calls njs_set_invalid(&deleted->start[i]) to properly initialize those gap entries. This prevents a later segmentation fault in njs_value_own_enumerate when iterating the deleted array. The finding describes a completely different issue (heap buffer overflow via memcpy) that is unrelated to the actual vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: When Array.prototype.splice() was called with a non-fast-array 'this' argument that had gaps (missing properties), and the 'deleted' result array was a fast array, the entries in deleted->start corresponding to the gaps were left uninitialized. Later access to these uninitialized entries (e.g., via njs_value_own_enumerate) caused a segmentation violation. The fix initializes gap entries in the deleted array with njs_set_invalid().

cve_desc: Use After Free in GitHub repository vim/vim prior to 8.2.

undesired_operation.description: When the reallocation of the 'files' array (bigger) fails, the code proceeds to access files[depth + 1] which is out-of-bounds.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing an attacker who controls included file paths (e.g., via crafted source files with deeply nested #include directives) to write past the allocated 'files' array, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

judgments.reasoning: The actual CVE-2022-1968 is a use-after-free issue where `ml_get()` returns a pointer into an internal buffer that can be freed/reallocated during subsequent operations, leaving `line` as a dangling pointer. The fix replaces `ml_get()` calls with `get_line_and_copy()` to copy the line into a stable buffer. This finding describes a completely different issue (potential heap buffer overflow in the files array reallocation logic), which is not what the CVE addresses.

actual_issue_summary: Use-after-free vulnerability where `line` is set to the return value of `ml_get()`, which returns a pointer to internal buffer memory that can be freed/reallocated by subsequent operations (like regex matching or file operations). The fix copies the line content into the `file_line` buffer using `get_line_and_copy()` instead of using the raw `ml_get()` pointer, preventing use of freed memory.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an end-to-end open source platform for machine learning. In affected versions the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv` is vulnerable to a division by 0 error. The [implementation](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/8d72537c6abf5a44103b57b9c2e22c14f5f49698/tensorflow/core/kernels/resource_variable_ops.cc#L865) uses a common class for all binary operations but fails to treat the division by 0 case separately. We have patched the issue in GitHub commit 4aacb30888638da75023e6601149415b39763d76. The fix will be included in TensorFlow 2.6.0. We will also cherrypick this commit on TensorFlow 2.5.1, TensorFlow 2.4.3, and TensorFlow 2.3.4, as these are also affected and still in supported range.

undesired_operation.description: params-> dim_ size(0) is call ed without first verifying that params has at least 1 dimension. If params is a rank-0 (scalar) tensor, dim_size(0) accesses an out-of-bounds dimension index, leading to undefined behavior.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash or potential information disclosure.

dim_size(0) is called on a rank-0 tensor

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is specifically about division by zero when ResourceScatterDiv receives updates containing zero values. The fix adds a check that updates must not contain 0 when the operation is DIV. This finding describes a different hypothetical issue about accessing dim_size(0) on a scalar tensor, which is unrelated to the division-by-zero vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The implementation of tf.raw_ops.ResourceScatterDiv did not validate that the updates tensor contains no zero values before performing division. An attacker could pass updates containing 0, causing a floating-point exception (division by zero). The fix adds a check that rejects updates containing 0 when the scatter operation is DIV on CPU.

cve_desc: TensorFlow is an open source platform for machine learning. Prior to versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4, the implementation of `tf.raw_ops.EditDistance` has incomplete validation. Users can pass negative values to cause a segmentation fault based denial of service. In multiple places throughout the code, one may compute an index for a write operation. However, the existing validation only checks against the upper bound of the array. Hence, it is possible to write before the array by massaging the input to generate negative values for `loc`. Versions 2.9.0, 2.8.1, 2.7.2, and 2.6.4 contain a patch for this issue.
