actual_issue_summary: The stack buffer 'buffer[64]' is too small to hold BMP Version 4 (biSize=108) or Version 5 (biSize=124) headers. When ReadOK reads biSize-4 bytes (104 or 120) into the 64-byte buffer, it overflows. The fix increases the buffer size from 64 to 128 bytes.

cve_desc: In ImageMagick 7.0.8-40 Q16, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the function WritePNMImage of coders/pnm.c, which allows an attacker to cause a denial of service or possibly information disclosure via a crafted image file. This is related to SetGrayscaleImage in MagickCore/quantize.c.

undesired_operation.description: When image->colors is 0 (e.g., a 0-row or 0-column image in the non-PseudoClass path), AcquireQuantumMemory(0, sizeof(*colormap)) may return a non-NULL pointer to a zero-size heap allocation. The code then writes a full PixelInfo struct to colormap[0], causing a heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing potential arbitrary code execution or denial of service via crash. An attacker provides a crafted image with 0 rows/columns to trigger the write of a PixelInfo struct (~80+ bytes) into a zero-size heap allocation.

step_by_step_execution.description: colormap allocated with image->colors (0) elements

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: AcquireQuantumMemory(0,...) may return non-NULL for zero-size allocation

step_by_step_execution.description: Write to colormap[0] via colormap[j]=image->colormap[0]

out_state.value: written with PixelInfo data beyond allocation bounds

judgments.reasoning: The CVE is about a heap-based buffer over-read (CWE-125) caused by the colormap_index array being too small. When ScaleQuantumToMap() returns an index larger than the allocated colormap_index size, it reads out of bounds. The fix ensures colormap_index is allocated with at least MaxMap elements. The finding describes a different hypothetical issue about zero-size colormap allocation, which is not what the CVE or the fix addresses.

actual_issue_summary: The colormap_index array in SetGrayscaleImage was allocated with size image->colors+1 (PseudoClass) or MaxColormapSize+1, but later accessed with indices from ScaleQuantumToMap() which can return values up to MaxMap. When MaxMap exceeds the allocated size, this causes a heap-based buffer over-read. The fix ensures the allocation is at least MaxMap elements by using MagickMax().

cve_desc: Rizin is a UNIX-like reverse engineering framework and command-line toolset. In versions up to and including 0.3.1 there is a heap-based out of bounds write in parse_die() when reversing an AMD64 ELF binary with DWARF debug info. When a malicious AMD64 ELF binary is opened by a victim user, Rizin may crash or execute unintended actions. No workaround are known and users are advised to upgrade.

undesired_operation.description: When abbrev->count is 0, the expression `abbrev->count - 1` underflows to SIZE_MAX (since size_t is unsigned), causing the loop to iterate ~2^64 times and access die->attr_values[i] and abbrev->defs[i] massively out of bounds.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read and write on die->attr_values and abbrev->defs arrays, leading to crash or potential arbitrary code execution when processing a crafted DWARF binary.

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop condition is evaluated: i < abbrev->count - 1. Since abbrev->count is 0 (size_t, unsigned), abbrev->count - 1 underflows to SIZE_MAX.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: 0 < SIZE_MAX is true, so the loop body executes

step_by_step_execution.description: memset writes to die->attr_values[i] which is out of bounds for large i values, and parse_attr_value reads from abbrev->defs[i] which is also out of bounds.

out_state.value: written out of bounds via memset

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2021-43814: when abbrev->count is 0, the unsigned subtraction underflows causing a massive loop that results in heap-based out-of-bounds writes. The fix adds an `if (abbrev->count)` guard before the loop.

actual_issue_summary: When abbrev->count is 0 in parse_die(), the unsigned expression `abbrev->count - 1` underflows to SIZE_MAX, causing a massive loop iteration that performs heap-based out-of-bounds writes via memset on die->attr_values[i]. The fix wraps the loop in an `if (abbrev->count)` check.

cve_desc: Stack consumption vulnerability in GD in PHP before 5.6.12 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service via a crafted imagefilltoborder call.

undesired_operation.description: When x >= im->sx, the left-seeking loop starts with i = x which is beyond the image width, causing gdImageGetPixel(im, i, y) and gdImageSetPixel(im, i, y, color) to access pixels beyond the row boundary.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read and write past the end of a pixel row. An attacker controlling x can corrupt adjacent memory or leak data.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: border is a valid non-negative color index

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop starts with i = x where x >= im->sx

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: x >= im->sx >= 1, so i >= 0 is true

step_by_step_execution.description: gdImageGetPixel(im, i, y) accesses pixel at out-of-bounds x coordinate

judgments.reasoning: The finding identifies a real issue fixed by the commit: missing bounds validation of x (and y) before use. The fix adds 'if (x >= im->sx) x = im->sx - 1' and 'if (y >= im->sy) y = im->sy - 1'. While the CVE emphasizes stack consumption from deep recursion, the out-of-bounds access from unclamped coordinates is part of the same bug (bug #22965) and the same fix. The OOB coordinates could also contribute to the stack consumption by causing the recursive fill to operate on unintended memory, preventing proper termination. This is a valid component of the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The vulnerable gdImageFillToBorder function lacks bounds checking on the x and y parameters. When called with x >= im->sx or y >= im->sy, out-of-bounds memory access occurs, and crafted inputs can cause unbounded recursion leading to stack consumption and denial of service. The fix clamps x and y to valid image dimensions before processing.

cve_desc: A null pointer de-reference was found in the way samba kerberos server handled missing sname in TGS-REQ (Ticket Granting Server - Request). An authenticated user could use this flaw to crash the samba server.

undesired_operation.description: In the need_referral path, krb5_make_principal return value is not checked. If it fails, sp remains NULL (explicitly set to NULL before), and the subsequent krb5_unparse_name dereferences a NULL pointer.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via NULL pointer dereference crash of the KDC process when processing a TGS-REQ for a service principal that triggers a referral via need_referral().

initial_state.value: set to a valid principal earlier
could be any value including NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: sp is freed and set to NULL before krb5_make_principal call

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If memory allocation fails inside krb5_make_principal, sp remains NULL

step_by_step_execution.description: krb5_unparse_name is called with NULL sp

judgments.reasoning: The CVE-2021-3671 is specifically about a missing NULL check on b->sname (the server name in TGS-REQ). When sname is absent from the request and enc_tkt_in_skey is not set, `s` remains NULL and `*s` is dereferenced. The fix adds an `else if (s == NULL)` check. The finding describes a different, theoretical issue about an unchecked return value of krb5_make_principal in the need_referral error-handling path, which is unrelated to the actual CVE.

actual_issue_summary: When a TGS-REQ is received without an sname (server name) field and enc_tkt_in_skey is not set, the pointer `s` (assigned from `b->sname`) remains NULL. The code then dereferences it via `*s` in the call to `_krb5_principalname2krb5_principal(context, &sp, *s, r)`, causing a NULL pointer dereference crash. The fix adds a check for `s == NULL` before the dereference.

cve_desc: Heap Buffer Overflow in parseDragons in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.8.

undesired_operation.description: memcmp reads 4 bytes starting at b+8 (and the first memcmp reads 4 bytes from b), but the buffer b may have been allocated with as few as 1 byte. This is a heap buffer over-read.
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of heap contents or crash (denial of service). An attacker who controls the binary file being parsed can trigger an out-of-bounds read.

step_by_step_execution.description: size is computed as r_buf_size(buf) and checked against off
size -= off reduces the remaining bytes

out_state.value: r_buf_size(buf) - off, could be as small as 1

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: size is at least 1 so the !size check passes

step_by_step_execution.description: b = malloc(size) allocates a buffer that could be very small (e.g., 1 byte)

out_state.value: heap buffer of 'size' bytes, possibly as small as 1

step_by_step_execution.description: memcmp reads 4 bytes from b and 4 bytes from b+8, both out of bounds if size < 12

out_state.value: heap buffer read out of bounds

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same root cause as CVE-2022-1061: the buffer b can be too small for the accesses performed on it (memcmp at b+8, r_read_le32 at b+12, etc.). The fix adds a 'size < 32' check to prevent this. While the CVE is classified as CWE-787 and the finding says CWE-125, both describe the same missing size validation vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: The parseDragons function did not check that the remaining buffer size was at least 32 bytes before performing reads at offsets like b+8 and b+12, leading to heap buffer overflows/over-reads on small inputs. The fix adds a 'size < 32' early return and changes the size type to st64 for proper signed comparison.

cve_desc: There's a flaw in jasper's jpc encoder in versions prior to 2.0.23. Crafted input provided to jasper by an attacker could cause an arbitrary out-of-bounds write. This could potentially affect data confidentiality, integrity, or application availability.

undesired_operation.description: The loop `for (rlvlno = 0; rlvlno < tccp->maxrlvls; ++rlvlno)` writes to `tccp->prcwidthexpns[rlvlno]` and `tccp->prcheightexpns[rlvlno]` using a user-controlled `maxrlvls` value before any upper-bound validation. The arrays are fixed-size (typically JPC_MAXRLVLS, e.g. 33 elements), but `maxrlvls` can be set to an arbitrarily large value via the OPT_MAXRLVLS option, causing an out-of-bounds write.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow allowing an attacker to corrupt adjacent memory, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution or denial of service.

state.value: iterates from 0 to tccp->maxrlvls - 1, exceeding array bounds

step_by_step_execution.description: tccp->maxrlvls is set to default value of 6
OPT_MAXRLVLS option is parsed from attacker-controlled input, setting maxrlvls to an arbitrary large value via atoi()

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: With tccp->maxrlvls set to a large value like 1000, the loop iterates far beyond the fixed-size array bounds of prcwidthexpns and prcheightexpns (sized JPC_MAXRLVLS, typically 33)

step_by_step_execution.description: Out-of-bounds writes to tccp->prcwidthexpns[rlvlno] and tccp->prcheightexpns[rlvlno] for rlvlno >= JPC_MAXRLVLS

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly matches the CVE. The fix adds an upper-bound check `if(tccp->maxrlvls > JPC_MAXRLVLS)` right after parsing the OPT_MAXRLVLS option, preventing the out-of-bounds write in the loop that uses maxrlvls as the iteration bound for writing into fixed-size arrays.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2020-27828: Missing upper-bound validation on `tccp->maxrlvls` when set via the OPT_MAXRLVLS encoder option allows an attacker to provide a value exceeding JPC_MAXRLVLS, causing an out-of-bounds heap write in the loop that populates `tccp->prcwidthexpns[]` and `tccp->prcheightexpns[]` arrays. The fix adds a check `if(tccp->maxrlvls > JPC_MAXRLVLS)` immediately after parsing the option value.

cve_desc: A race condition exists in Eternal Terminal prior to version 6.2.0 that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization socket, enabling the attacker to login to other systems as the targeted users. The bug is in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId().

undesired_operation.description: strcpy copies pipePath (attacker/caller-controlled string) into fixed-size local.sun_path buffer (typically 108 bytes) without any length check, causing a stack buffer overflow
undesired_operation.impact: Stack-based buffer overflow leading to arbitrary code execution or crash. An attacker who controls the endpoint name can overwrite the return address and other stack variables.

state.value: attacker-controlled string potentially longer than 108 bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: pipePath is set from endpoint.name()

out_state.value: string of arbitrary length from endpoint.name()

step_by_step_execution.description: strcpy copies pipePath into local.sun_path without bounds checking

out_state.value: overflowed if pipePath exceeds buffer size

judgments.reasoning: CVE-2022-24950 is a race condition in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId() that allows SSH authorization socket hijacking. The finding identifies a buffer overflow (strcpy→strncpy), which is a different bug that happened to be fixed in the same commit. The strcpy fix is a hardening change, not the race condition vulnerability described by the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: CVE-2022-24950 is a race condition in UserTerminalRouter::getInfoForId() that allows an authenticated attacker to hijack other users' SSH authorization sockets. The commit contained multiple fixes; the diff shown here (strcpy→strncpy in PipeSocketHandler::listen) is a separate buffer overflow hardening change included in the same commit, not the core race condition fix.

cve_desc: The GNU C Library (aka glibc or libc6) before 2.27 contains an off-by-one error leading to a heap-based buffer overflow in the glob function in glob.c, related to the processing of home directories using the ~ operator followed by a long string.

undesired_operation.description: When handling tilde expansion for ~username/path patterns, the buffer allocated for user_name is 'end_name - dirname' bytes, but 'end_name - dirname' bytes are copied from 'dirname + 1' followed by a null terminator write, resulting in a 1-byte out-of-bounds write (off-by-one).
undesired_operation.impact: Heap or stack buffer overflow by one byte. On heap allocations (malloc_user_name = 1), this corrupts heap metadata, potentially leading to arbitrary code execution. On stack (alloca), it corrupts adjacent stack data.

state.value: end_name - dirname bytes (should be end_name - dirname - 1)

initial_state.value: points to pattern string starting with '~', e.g. "~username/path"
points to '/' in dirname (result of strchr(dirname, '/'))
NULL (no backslash found, taking else branch)
unset, will be allocated with size end_name - dirname

step_by_step_execution.description: dirname is set to pattern like '~username/path', end_name = strchr(dirname, '/') points to the '/'

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: end_name points to '/' so it is not NULL, entering the else branch that allocates newp

step_by_step_execution.description: newp is allocated with size (end_name - dirname) bytes. For '~username', that's 9 bytes.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: No backslash in dirname before end_name, so unescape is NULL, taking the else branch with the buggy mempcpy

step_by_step_execution.description: mempcpy copies (end_name - dirname) = 9 bytes from dirname+1 ('username/') into newp (9-byte buffer), then writes '\0' at position newp+9, which is 1 byte past the buffer end

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2017-15670: an off-by-one error in glob.c where mempcpy copies end_name - dirname bytes instead of end_name - dirname - 1 bytes, causing a 1-byte heap/stack buffer overflow. The diff confirms the fix changes exactly this expression.

actual_issue_summary: Off-by-one error in glob() tilde expansion: when copying the username from a ~username/path pattern without escape characters, mempcpy copies (end_name - dirname) bytes from dirname+1 instead of (end_name - dirname - 1) bytes, writing the null terminator one byte past the allocated buffer. The fix changes the copy length to end_name - dirname - 1.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability was found in the Linux kernel Intel’s iSMT SMBus host controller driver in the way it handled the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case (via the ioctl I2C_SMBUS) with malicious input data. This flaw could allow a local user to crash the system.

undesired_operation.description: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case, data->block[0] is used without bounds checking to determine the memcpy size, allowing a write past the end of the dma_buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Buffer overflow in kernel memory leading to potential code execution or denial of service.

step_by_step_execution.description: Function enters the switch statement with size == I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The user triggers the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case via ioctl I2C_SMBUS

step_by_step_execution.description: memcpy copies data->block[0] bytes from data->block[1] into dma_buffer[1] without checking data->block[0] against I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX

out_state.value: written with up to 255 bytes, overflowing buffer

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2022-3077: the missing bounds check on data->block[0] in the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case allows a buffer overflow via memcpy. The fix adds exactly this check.

actual_issue_summary: In the I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_PROC_CALL case of ismt_access(), data->block[0] (user-controlled, 0-255) was used directly as the memcpy length without being validated against I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX (32), allowing writes beyond the end of dma_buffer. The fix adds a bounds check returning -EINVAL if data->block[0] > I2C_SMBUS_BLOCK_MAX.

cve_desc: Curl versions 7.14.1 through 7.61.1 are vulnerable to a heap-based buffer over-read in the tool_msgs.c:voutf() function that may result in information exposure and denial of service.

undesired_operation.description: When a word longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1 characters (no spaces) is in the buffer and len > WARN_TEXTWIDTH, the variable 'cut' reaches 0. Then 'len -= cut' subtracts 0, so len never decreases, while 'ptr += cut+1' advances ptr by 1 each iteration. This causes an infinite loop that reads memory far beyond the stack buffer print_buffer[256].
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via infinite loop, and out-of-bounds read that leaks stack memory contents to config->errors output stream. An attacker who controls the formatted string content can trigger both.

initial_state.value: return value of vsnprintf, representing length of formatted string

step_by_step_execution.description: vsnprintf formats the string into print_buffer and returns len

out_state.value: formatted string with a long word (no spaces) exceeding WARN_TEXTWIDTH

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: len is positive after vsnprintf
The formatted string is longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH

step_by_step_execution.description: cut is initialized to WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1, then the while loop decrements cut looking for a space. With no spaces, cut reaches 0.
len -= cut subtracts 0, so len is unchanged. ptr += cut+1 advances ptr by only 1 byte.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: len was not decremented, so loop continues indefinitely, eventually ptr goes past print_buffer boundary

relevant_state.value: past print_buffer bounds after enough iterations

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly describes the CVE-2018-16842 vulnerability: when no space is found, cut becomes 0, len never decreases, and ptr advances past the buffer, causing a heap/stack buffer over-read and effective infinite loop. The fix adds 'if(0 == cut) cut = WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1;' to prevent exactly this scenario.

actual_issue_summary: In warnf(), when a word longer than WARN_TEXTWIDTH has no spaces, the cut variable reaches 0. This means len -= cut never decreases len, creating an infinite loop where ptr advances one byte at a time past the end of the 256-byte stack buffer print_buffer, causing an out-of-bounds read (CWE-125). The fix resets cut to WARN_TEXTWIDTH-1 when no space is found.

cve_desc: The php_wddx_pop_element function in ext/wddx/wddx.c in PHP before 5.6.25 and 7.x before 7.0.10 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (NULL pointer dereference and application crash) or possibly have unspecified other impact via an invalid base64 binary value, as demonstrated by a wddx_deserialize call that mishandles a binary element in a wddxPacket XML document.

undesired_operation.description: php_base64_decode can return NULL on invalid input, and the return value is stored directly into Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) without a NULL check. Subsequent operations that dereference this string will dereference a NULL pointer.
undesired_operation.impact: Denial of service via crash (NULL pointer dereference). An attacker can craft a WDDX packet with an invalid base64 binary element to crash the PHP process.

state.value: NULL (returned by php_base64_decode on invalid base64 input)

initial_state.value: non-NULL zval containing a string with invalid base64 content

step_by_step_execution.description: The element name matches EL_BINARY, entering the binary processing block
php_base64_decode is called with invalid base64 data and returns NULL
STR_FREE frees the old string value of ent1->data
NULL is assigned to Z_STRVAL_P(ent1->data) without any check

judgments.reasoning: This finding exactly matches CVE-2016-7130: the NULL return from php_base64_decode on invalid base64 input is assigned to the zval string without a NULL check, leading to a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds exactly this NULL check.

actual_issue_summary: php_base64_decode can return NULL when given invalid base64 input. In the EL_BINARY handling code of php_wddx_pop_element, the return value was assigned directly to the zval string pointer without checking for NULL, causing a NULL pointer dereference. The fix adds a NULL check and sets an empty string if decoding fails.

cve_desc: st21nfca_connectivity_event_received in drivers/nfc/st21nfca/se.c in the Linux kernel through 5.16.12 has EVT_TRANSACTION buffer overflows because of untrusted length parameters.

undesired_operation.description: The logical AND (&&) in the validation check should be OR (||). As written, a packet with the correct AID tag (0x81) but a length shorter than NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2 will bypass the check, leading to out-of-bounds reads on skb->data when accessing indices 1, 2, etc.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read from skb->data when skb is too short, potentially leaking kernel heap memory or causing a crash

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: skb->len < NFC_MIN_AID_LENGTH + 2 is true, but skb->data[0] == NFC_EVT_TRANSACTION_AID_TAG so second condition is false; AND yields false, check is bypassed

step_by_step_execution.description: Code proceeds to access skb->data[1] for aid_len despite skb being too short

judgments.reasoning: While the && vs || logic issue is arguably a real bug, CVE-2022-26490 specifically concerns buffer overflows caused by untrusted length parameters (aid_len and params_len from skb->data) being passed directly to memcpy without bounds checking. The fix adds validation that aid_len <= sizeof(transaction->aid) and params_len <= allocated size. The finding describes a different issue (the initial length/tag validation logic) that was NOT addressed by the CVE fix.

actual_issue_summary: Buffer overflows in EVT_TRANSACTION handling: skb->data[1] (aid_len) and skb->data[aid_len+3] (params_len) are used directly as memcpy length parameters without bounds checks, allowing writes beyond the transaction->aid and transaction->params buffers. The fix adds checks that aid_len <= sizeof(transaction->aid) and params_len <= remaining allocated size.

cve_desc: libmobi is vulnerable to Out-of-bounds Write

undesired_operation.description: When offset is negative, there is no check that buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen before the memmove and subsequent offset advance. This allows writing past the end of the allocated buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker can overwrite memory beyond the buffer boundary, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution or causing a crash.

initial_state.value: size_t provided by caller, potentially large

step_by_step_execution.description: aoffset is computed as abs(offset)
source is set to buf->data + buf->offset

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: offset is negative, so we enter the else branch
buf->offset (e.g. 10) >= aoffset (e.g. 1), so the bounds check passes. Crucially, there is no check that buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen.

step_by_step_execution.description: source is decremented by aoffset
memmove writes len bytes from source to buf->data + buf->offset, writing past buf->maxlen

out_state.value: len bytes written, overflowing past buf->maxlen

judgments.reasoning: The finding precisely identifies the missing bounds check in the negative offset branch: buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen is not checked, allowing an out-of-bounds write via memmove. This is exactly what the fix adds and matches CVE-2021-3751 (CWE-787, out-of-bounds write).

actual_issue_summary: In mobi_buffer_move, when offset is negative, the function only checks that buf->offset >= aoffset (to prevent reading before the buffer start) but does not check that buf->offset + len <= buf->maxlen. This allows memmove to write past the end of the allocated buffer. The fix adds the missing check (buf->offset + len > buf->maxlen) to the negative offset branch.

cve_desc: A buffer overflow vulnerability in jetp3852_print_page() in devices/gdev3852.c of Artifex Software GhostScript v9.50 allows a remote attacker to escalate privileges via a crafted PDF file. This is fixed in v9.51.

undesired_operation.description: memset writes 7 bytes past the end of the stack-allocated 'data' buffer when line_size is close to or equal to DATA_SIZE and the scan line has non-zero trailing bytes
undesired_operation.impact: Stack buffer overflow — attacker who controls page dimensions/resolution can overwrite adjacent stack variables (plane_data or saved registers/return address), potentially achieving code execution or denial of service

state.value: data + DATA_SIZE (pointing at or near end of buffer)

initial_state.value: uninitialized stack buffer of DATA_SIZE bytes

step_by_step_execution.description: line_size is computed from device properties

out_state.value: value from gdev_mem_bytes_per_scan_line, up to DATA_SIZE

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: line_size equals DATA_SIZE, so the check passes and execution continues

step_by_step_execution.description: end_data is set to data + line_size
Scan line data is copied into data buffer

out_state.value: filled with scan line content up to line_size bytes

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: If the last byte of scan data is non-zero, end_data stays at data + DATA_SIZE
end_data is data + DATA_SIZE, which is not equal to data, so this is not a blank line

step_by_step_execution.description: memset(end_data, 0, 7) writes 7 zero bytes starting at data + DATA_SIZE, overflowing the stack buffer

in_state.value: data + DATA_SIZE (one past end of buffer)

out_state.value: 0 (written out of bounds on the stack)

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies a buffer overflow in the vulnerable code. When line_size == DATA_SIZE and the scan line has non-zero trailing bytes, end_data remains at data + DATA_SIZE, and the memset(end_data, 0, 7) writes 7 bytes past the stack buffer. This memset was removed by the fix. The CVE is described as a buffer overflow in jetp3852_print_page() (CWE-120), and this finding identifies a real overflow component in the code that was changed. While the commit message emphasizes uninitialized bytes used as array indices (the other aspect of the fix), the removal of this dangerous memset was part of the same fix, and the overflow it causes is a valid component of the CVE.

actual_issue_summary: The data[] stack buffer was not fully initialized. When line_size < DATA_SIZE, bytes beyond line_size were uninitialized and could contain values > 7, which were then used as indices into 8-element lookup arrays (spr40[], spr8[], spr2[]), causing out-of-bounds reads. The old code attempted a partial fix with memset(end_data, 0, 7) which only zeroed 7 bytes (and could itself overflow the buffer). The fix initializes the entire data[] buffer to zero at the start and removes the inadequate 7-byte memset.

cve_desc: In Exiv2 0.26, there is a heap-based buffer over-read in the Exiv2::Image::byteSwap4 function in image.cpp. Remote attackers can exploit this vulnerability to disclose memory data or cause a denial of service via a crafted TIFF file.

undesired_operation.description: When type is rational (size=8) and count overflows size*count to a small value, the buffer allocated is small but the loop iterates kount=5 times accessing offsets up to 4*8+4=36 bytes in a buffer that may be as small as 28 bytes, causing heap buffer over-read that leaks heap memory to output.
code_snippets:
uint32_t a = byteSwap4(buf,k*size+0,bSwap);
                            uint32_t b = byteSwap4(buf,k*size+4,bSwap);
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure - heap memory contents leaked to output stream

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: a crafted TIFF file can cause size*count to be smaller than expected (via integer overflow or a large crafted count), resulting in an undersized buffer allocation. Subsequent accesses via byteSwap4 then read beyond the buffer bounds. This matches CVE-2017-17723 (heap-based buffer over-read in byteSwap4) and the fix, which adds a check that the allocation size doesn't exceed io.size() before allocating the buffer.

actual_issue_summary: A crafted TIFF file can specify a large 'count' value in an IFD entry, causing the buffer allocation size (size*count + pad+20) to be insufficient or to overflow. Subsequent reads via byteSwap4 access beyond the allocated buffer, causing a heap-based buffer over-read. The fix adds a bounds check ensuring the allocation size doesn't exceed the file size before allocating the buffer.

cve_desc: Heap-based Buffer Overflow in GitHub repository radareorg/radare2 prior to 5.6.2.

undesired_operation.description: R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset) reads 2 bytes at offset 6 from buffer without checking that sz >= 8, causing an out-of-bounds read when sz < 8
undesired_operation.impact: Information disclosure of heap/stack contents or crash (denial of service) — attacker provides a truncated Java class attribute with sz < 8

initial_state.value: pointer to attacker-supplied data of size sz

step_by_step_execution.description: offset is incremented by 6 after calling r_bin_java_default_attr_new

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Assuming r_bin_java_default_attr_new succeeds and returns a valid attr

step_by_step_execution.description: R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT(buffer, offset) reads 2 bytes at buffer+6 without verifying sz >= 8

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the missing bounds check before reading from the buffer at offset 6. The fix adds exactly `if (buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz) return NULL;` before this read. The commit message itself says 'Fix oobread segfault', confirming this is an out-of-bounds read. While the CVE is classified as CWE-787 (heap-based buffer overflow), the actual fix and commit message align with the OOB read described. The finding identifies the same root cause vulnerability.

actual_issue_summary: Missing bounds check before reading from buffer at offset 6. The fix adds `if (buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz) return NULL;` before the first R_BIN_JAVA_USHORT read and also fixes the loop bounds check to include buf_offset (changing `offset + 8 > sz` to `buf_offset + offset + 8 > sz`), preventing out-of-bounds access on truncated Java class files.

cve_desc: LibVNC before commit 7b1ef0ffc4815cab9a96c7278394152bdc89dc4d contains heap out-of-bound write vulnerability inside structure in VNC client code that can result remote code execution

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in the expression `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` allows the size check against RFB_BUFFER_SIZE to be bypassed. A large hdr.nSubrects causes the multiplication to wrap around to a small value, so only a small amount of data is read into client->buffer, but the subsequent loop iterates hdr.nSubrects times, reading far past the end of client->buffer.
code_snippets: for (i = 0; i < hdr.nSubrects; i++) { pix = *(CARDBPP *)ptr; ptr += BPP/8; x = *ptr++; y = *ptr++; w = *ptr++; h = *ptr++; }
undesired_operation.impact: Heap-based out-of-bounds read from client->buffer, leading to information disclosure or crash. A malicious VNC server can trigger this against a connecting client.

state.value: A large value chosen so that hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8)) wraps around to a small value

step_by_step_execution.description: hdr.nSubrects is read from server and byte-swapped

out_state.value: attacker-controlled large value, e.g. chosen so multiplication overflows

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: Due to integer overflow, the multiplication wraps to a small value that is <= RFB_BUFFER_SIZE

step_by_step_execution.description: ReadFromRFBServer reads the (small) wrapped amount of data into client->buffer

out_state.value: contains small amount of data read from server

step_by_step_execution.description: Loop iterates hdr.nSubrects times, reading past the end of client->buffer

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the root cause of CVE-2018-20020: the integer overflow in `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8))` that bypasses the buffer size check. The fix changes the check to use division instead of multiplication to avoid overflow. The finding labels it as out-of-bounds read (CWE-125) while the CVE says out-of-bounds write (CWE-787) — the loop both reads from the buffer out-of-bounds and then passes data to GotFillRect which writes — but the core vulnerability identified is the same.

actual_issue_summary: Integer overflow in the buffer size check `hdr.nSubrects * (4 + (BPP / 8)) > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE` allows the check to be bypassed when hdr.nSubrects is large enough to cause the multiplication to wrap around. The fix changes the check to `hdr.nSubrects > RFB_BUFFER_SIZE / (4 + (BPP / 8))` to avoid integer overflow, preventing heap out-of-bounds access in the subsequent loop.

cve_desc: A denial of service vulnerability was found in rsyslog in the imptcp module. An attacker could send a specially crafted message to the imptcp socket, which would cause rsyslog to crash. Versions before 8.27.0 are vulnerable.

undesired_operation.description: Integer overflow in iOctetsRemain accumulation (iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0') can produce a negative value. This negative value is then assigned to octatesToCopy (an int), which is passed to memcpy as size_t, becoming an extremely large value, causing a massive heap buffer overflow.
undesired_operation.impact: Remote code execution or denial of service — attacker sends a crafted TCP syslog message with a very long digit sequence for the octet count, overflowing iOctetsRemain to a negative value, which becomes a huge size_t in memcpy, overwriting heap memory far beyond pMsg buffer.

state.value: negative int, interpreted as huge size_t by memcpy

step_by_step_execution.description: First digit character arrives, inputState transitions to eInOctetCnt, iOctetsRemain set to 0

Attacker sends many digit characters repeatedly. Each call accumulates: iOctetsRemain = iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0'. After enough digits (e.g., sending '9999999999...'), the int overflows past INT_MAX and wraps to a negative value.

out_state.value: negative value due to signed integer overflow

step_by_step_execution.description: A space character arrives, terminating the octet count. inputState transitions to eInMsg.

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: iOctetsRemain is negative, which is < 1, so the error is logged but execution continues — no abort or state reset occurs in the vulnerable version

step_by_step_execution.description: On next data character, the OCTET_COUNTING branch is taken. octatesToCopy = pThis->iOctetsRemain (negative). Then memcpy is called with this value cast to size_t, causing a massive read/write.

out_state.value: negative int, interpreted as huge size_t by memcpy

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the core vulnerability: unbounded accumulation in iOctetsRemain leads to integer overflow (CWE-190), which can result in a crash (denial of service). The fix adds a bound check (iOctetsRemain <= 200000000) to prevent the overflow. This matches CVE-2018-16881 exactly.

actual_issue_summary: In rsyslog's imptcp module, the octet count parser (iOctetsRemain = iOctetsRemain * 10 + c - '0') had no upper bound check, allowing an attacker to send a message with an extremely large digit sequence that causes integer overflow. The fix adds a check that iOctetsRemain <= 200000000 before continuing accumulation, and also fixes related issues like switching to octet stuffing on invalid counts and resetting iMsg.

cve_desc: MariaDB Server v10.6 and below was discovered to contain an use-after-free in the component my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exploited via specially crafted SQL statements.

undesired_operation.description: When dl->length is less than so_ext_len (e.g., sizeof(SO_EXT)-1, typically 3), the expression 'dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len' wraps around (pointer arithmetic underflow), causing my_strcasecmp to read memory before dl->str buffer.
undesired_operation.impact: Out-of-bounds read before the dl->str buffer, potentially leaking sensitive heap/stack data or causing a crash (denial of service).

state.value: less than so_ext_len (e.g., 0, 1, or 2 when SO_EXT is '.so')

initial_state.value: attacker-controlled, can be any value including 0
pointer to dynamic library name string

step_by_step_execution.description: so_ext_len is computed as sizeof(SO_EXT) - 1, typically 3

step_by_step_execution.branch_taken: false (no check exists in vulnerable version)
step_by_step_execution.reasoning: In the vulnerable version there is no length check; execution proceeds directly to pointer arithmetic

step_by_step_execution.description: Pointer arithmetic dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len underflows, producing a pointer before dl->str
my_strcasecmp reads from the underflowed pointer, accessing memory before dl->str

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the same vulnerability fixed by the commit. The fix adds 'dl->length < so_ext_len' check to prevent the pointer arithmetic underflow that causes invalid memory access inside my_strcasecmp_8bit. While the CVE labels it 'use-after-free', the actual issue is an out-of-bounds/invalid memory access via pointer underflow in my_strcasecmp_8bit, which is exactly what this finding describes.

actual_issue_summary: When dl->length is smaller than so_ext_len, the expression 'dl->str + dl->length - so_ext_len' underflows, causing my_strcasecmp_8bit to access memory before the dl->str buffer. The fix adds a bounds check 'dl->length < so_ext_len' before performing the subtraction.

cve_desc: This affects the package juce-framework/JUCE before 6.1.5. This vulnerability is triggered when a malicious archive is crafted with an entry containing a symbolic link. When extracted, the symbolic link is followed outside of the target dir allowing writing arbitrary files on the target host. In some cases, this can allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code. The vulnerable code is in the ZipFile::uncompressEntry function in juce_ZipFile.cpp and is executed when the archive is extracted upon calling uncompressTo() on a ZipFile object.

undesired_operation.description: The entry filename from the zip archive is passed directly to getChildFile without sanitizing path traversal sequences like '../'. An attacker can craft a zip file with entries such as '../../etc/cron.d/evil' to write files outside the target directory.
undesired_operation.impact: Arbitrary file write outside the intended extraction directory, leading to potential remote code execution (e.g., overwriting executables, cron jobs, SSH keys, etc.)

state.value: Contains path traversal sequences like '../' allowing escape from targetDirectory

initial_state.value: unset, will be derived from zei->entry.filename

step_by_step_execution.description: entryPath is set from the zip entry filename, which is attacker-controlled

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The malicious path is non-empty

step_by_step_execution.description: targetFile is computed from targetDirectory and entryPath with no path traversal check

step_by_step_execution.reasoning: The path does not end with a separator, so it is treated as a file

step_by_step_execution.description: File content is written to the traversed path outside the target directory

judgments.reasoning: The finding correctly identifies the path traversal vulnerability that the fix addresses with the `isAChildOf` check. While the CVE description emphasizes symbolic links, the fix also adds a path traversal check (`!targetFile.isAChildOf(targetDirectory)`), and path traversal is a core component of this CVE. CWE-22 (Path Traversal) is closely related to CWE-59 (Symlink Following) — both are aspects of the same vulnerability allowing writes outside the target directory.

actual_issue_summary: The ZipFile::uncompressEntry function lacked two checks: (1) no validation that the resolved target file is actually inside the target directory (path traversal via '../' sequences), and (2) no check for symbolic links in the parent directory path that could redirect extraction outside the target directory. The fix adds an `isAChildOf` check for path traversal and a `hasSymbolicPart` check for symlink following.

cve_desc: The qtm_decompress function in libclamav/mspack.c in ClamAV before 0.96 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory corruption and application crash) via a crafted CAB archive that uses the Quantum (aka .Q) compression format.  NOTE: some of these details are obtained from third party information.

undesired_operation.description: In the match copy path when match_offset > window_posn (wrapping case), there is no check that window_posn + match_length <= window_size. The copy loop writes match_length bytes starting at &window[window_posn], which can overflow the window buffer if match_length is large enough that the write extends past the allocated window.
undesired_operation.impact: Heap buffer overflow — attacker-controlled compressed data can write past the end of the allocated window buffer, potentially achieving arbitrary code execution or causing a crash.

initial_state.value: restored from qtm->window_posn, some value < window_size
unset, determined by selector from compressed data
fixed size of allocated window buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Selector 6 is decoded from model7, giving a variable-length match
match_length is decoded from model6len: qtm->length_base[sym] + extra + 5, which can be large

out_state.value: large value, e.g. exceeding window_size - window_posn

step_by_step_execution.description: match_offset is decoded from model6, set to a value > window_posn to trigger wrapping path

step_by_step_execution.branch_taken: true (no check exists in vulnerable version)
step_by_step_execution.reasoning: There is no bounds check on window_posn + match_length before writing, so the copy proceeds past the end of the window buffer

step_by_step_execution.description: Copy loop writes match_length bytes starting at &window[window_posn], overflowing the buffer

out_state.value: past end of window buffer — heap corruption

judgments.reasoning: The finding exactly identifies the missing bounds check that the fix adds: window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size. This is precisely the vulnerability described in CVE-2010-1311 — a crafted CAB archive with Quantum compression can cause memory corruption due to the missing check before the match copy loop.

actual_issue_summary: The qtm_decompress function lacked a check that window_posn + match_length does not exceed window_size before performing the match copy. A crafted CAB archive could supply match parameters that cause writes beyond the window buffer, leading to memory corruption and crash. The fix adds an explicit check returning CL_EFORMAT if window_posn + match_length > qtm->window_size.
